user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) was correct in direction: fed_assets rose again to 8,860,485 from 8,867,834 one observation back — actually the latest print (8,860,485) is slightly below the prior observation (8,867,834), a small dip, but the broader 4-week and 13-week changes remain strongly positive (+103,025 and +304,304 respectively), and the level sits essentially at the 52-week high (only 7,349 below it). The policy language confirms continued net asset purchases, but at a tapering pace: Treasury purchases cut from $80B/month equivalent path down to $20B/month and MBS to $10B/month beginning [date] following a prior cut from $40B/$20B. This is still QE — assets are still being added, not drained — but the pace of expansion is decelerating sharply toward zero. Given tapering is explicit and nearly complete, the balance sheet is still growing but at a much slower rate, and the single most recent observation ticked down slightly, which could be noise or the first sign of the taper's effect. Net acquisition pace is small and shrinking, consistent with expansion continuing through month-end but at a diminishing rate, not yet flat or reversing.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing purchases reduced to zero/runoff, would flip this to flat or down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "flat" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: the spread has now broken decisively below 0.70, hitting a new 12-month low of 0.61, exactly the falsifier condition I set for renewed flattening. The last three readings (0.63, 0.63, 0.61) show flattening momentum has not stalled, with slope_change_21d at -0.18pp and slope_change_63d at -0.46pp both confirming a persistent, accelerating flattening trend rather than noise. The spread sits at slope_range_position 0.00 — the very bottom of its 12-month range — with slope_distance_from_flat down to just 0.61pp, meaning inversion risk is now much closer. Policy language has shifted hawkish: guidance moved from tapering-in-progress to "soon appropriate to raise the target range," which historically compresses front-end-to-belly spreads faster than the long end, consistent with the observed flattening. The 10s30s slope at +0.32pp remains modestly positive, suggesting the flattening pressure is concentrated in the 2s10s segment rather than a broad long-end selloff. With no signs of stabilization in the last three prints and hawkish rhetoric intensifying, I expect continued flattening into month-end, though the pace of decline may moderate somewhat given how compressed the spread already is.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.70 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_distance_from_flat, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially stabilized after its earlier tightening drift. Over the last 13 weeks the index rose modestly (+0.08) from very loose levels, but the most recent 4 weeks show a slight reversal (-0.01), with the last several prints clustered tightly between -0.54 and -0.56. The latest value (-0.55) sits right at the middle of this recent range, and the range_position of 0.90 shows we're near the high (tighter) end of the 52-week span, but momentum has flattened rather than continuing to build. My prior flat call was directionally correct — the anticipated resumption of a clear multi-week rise did not materialize, nor did a drop back toward -0.60. The series is oscillating in a narrow band with no clear trend. Policy language remains unchanged, describing conditions as "accommodative," consistent with the very negative distance-from-zero reading (-0.55), but this is stale boilerplate and not informative for the one-month change. Given the flattening momentum and lack of a decisive directional push in either the measurements or the policy text, I maintain a flat call, with slightly higher conviction than before since the range has now held for several consecutive weeks without breaking.
Would change its mind if: A break below -0.60 (renewed easing) or a sustained multi-week climb toward -0.50 or higher (renewed tightening) would change this call.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 10 breakevens (2.46→2.44) show stabilization after an early dip, not a renewed uptrend. The series bottomed near 2.33 mid-window, rebounded to 2.38-2.44, and has now printed flat for two consecutive readings (2.44, 2.44). My prior "up" call was wrong in direction of magnitude — the 21d and 63d changes are both modestly negative (-0.14, -0.13 pp), showing the medium-term trend is still net down from where it was a month/quarter ago, even though the most recent few prints have leveled off. The 252d change (+0.31pp) shows expectations remain well above where they were a year ago, and the range position (0.51) sits right in the middle of the 12-month band, arguing against a strong directional signal either way. Distance from target remains firmly above 2% (+0.44pp), consistent with expectations anchored moderately above objective, but that's a level story, not a momentum one. Given the flattening in the last two prints after the dip, and negative but decelerating short/medium-term momentum, I don't see strong evidence for continued upward drift. The most defensible call now is essentially flat, with a slight lean toward stabilization rather than a resumed rise.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.50 or below 2.35 sustained over multiple observations would confirm a genuine directional move rather than range-bound flatness.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.62
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose further to 7.56% from 7.17%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.21% from 4.89%, extending an unbroken multi-month acceleration. The falsifier I set (two consecutive sub-0.3% mom prints plus core PCE turning down) did not occur — mom actually reaccelerated: 0.86 → 0.69 → 0.60, still well above the 0.72% 3-month average and far above any level consistent with 2% annualized inflation. The 3m annualized headline pace (8.95%) is running hotter than the 12m y/y rate (7.56%), and core 3m annualized (6.64%) likewise exceeds its own y/y print, meaning momentum is not decelerating even though the single latest mom reading (0.60) is modestly below the immediately preceding two. Policy language confirms this isn't yet seen as easing: officials shifted from calling imbalances "transitory" to acknowledging inflation "has exceeded 2 percent for some time," and began tapering asset purchases — a clear acknowledgment of sustained overheating. Core is running far above target (+3.21pp) and the range position is at the top of its 12-month band (1.00). While the deceleration in mom from 0.86 to 0.60 is a mild yellow flag worth watching, it's not enough on its own to break the trend given still-elevated 3m annualized rates and rising y/y in both series. I still expect headline CPI y/y to rise next month.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the 12m y/y rate.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.00% from 3.90%, an uptick of 0.10pp after a long, steep decline (-2.40pp over 12 months). This confirms my prior falsifier condition — I said a print at or above 4.00-4.10% would signal stalling, and that's exactly what happened. The unemployment rate is now essentially at the 3m average (4.00%) and just 0.10pp above the 12-month low, so the Sahm-gap remains tiny (+0.10pp) and doesn't yet indicate a genuine trend reversal or rapid deterioration. However, the multi-month streak of consecutive declines has clearly broken — this is the first uptick after six straight prints of falling or flat readings. Policy language still describes "substantial" declines and "solid" job gains, but that language reflects the trend through the prior prints, not this newest uptick, and is now being used to justify tightening (tapering asset purchases), consistent with labor conditions being assessed as near maximum employment rather than still loosening rapidly. Given the level is at the natural rate (distance_from_natural ≈ 0) and the increase is small but real, I read this as a stalling of the tightening trend rather than a resumption of loosening — a flat-to-slightly-up call with modest conviction, not a confident reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.20% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.50pp would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than a stall near the natural rate.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_3m_avg

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated: dgs10 has climbed from 1.75 to 1.79 recently after a dip, with 21d change +0.27pp and 63d +0.22pp, both positive. The 12-month range position sits at 0.90, meaning the yield is near the top of its trailing range, and it's 0.70pp above its 12m low versus only 0.08pp below the 12m high. The 252d change of +0.68pp confirms a strong sustained uptrend over the past year. The 10s30s slope of +0.32pp is not inverted, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening from flight-to-quality. Policy language confirms continued tapering of Treasury purchases (now down to $20bn/month from $40bn), which reduces central bank demand for long-end paper and should keep upward pressure on term premium via reduced balance-sheet support. This is a incremental continuation of the previously announced taper, not a reversal, so it reinforces rather than changes the up call. Momentum has been choppy short-term (1.85→1.81→1.78→1.79) but the broader trend and level near range highs support continued modest upward pressure into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-session drop below 1.70 with negative 21d momentum would signal easing term-premium pressure and reverse this call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace and composition of future runoff/taper decisions directly drive long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could trigger flight-to-quality that would offset taper-driven upward pressure.

arbitrated.notes: Net read: still tilted toward modestly higher nominal yields, but conviction is lower than last meeting because the two drivers that actually move the front end and belly — inflation and labor — are pulling in different directions and one is stale. Inflation remains the strongest, freshest signal for repricing: CPI y/y and core PCE both accelerated further, mom is still running hot on a 3m-annualized basis, and policy language has shifted from "transitory" to explicit acknowledgment of persistent overshoot plus taper — this is a live, high-conviction driver that the market has not fully priced and it argues for higher yields. Labor tightness, however, is now stale (23 days) and its own signal has softened: the first uptick in unemployment after a long decline suggests the tightening trend is stalling, which is a mild disinflationary/dovish counterweight, though conviction there is modest and the print is still near the natural rate. Term premium's own read (up, 0.40) is directionally consistent with inflation and taper-driven reduced Fed demand for long-end paper, which is exactly the transmission mechanism from balance-sheet tapering to higher yields at the long end — this reinforces rather than duplicates the inflation signal, so I lean on both. Financial conditions and balance sheet are slow, well-anticipated, largely-priced drivers: FCI is genuinely flat/range-bound (not stale, but the driver itself is low-informational for month-ahead repricing), and balance sheet, while still expanding, is explicitly decelerating toward flat — a continuation, not a regime change. Curve slope is out of scope for this seat. Overall: inflation dominates, term premium corroborates via the taper-supply channel, labor is a modest and stale offset. I maintain a modestly bullish-yield (higher-yield) stance but trim conviction versus last meeting given labor's softening and FCI's genuine flatness.

Slow-moving, largely anticipated taper continuation; not fresh news for level repricing this horizon.
Curve pod's driver on real/nominal split, out of scope for this seat's directional level view.

arbitrated.falsifier: A sub-0.3% CPI mom print with core PCE y/y turning down, or unemployment breaking above 4.20%/Sahm-gap >0.50pp, would flip this toward flat/down.

risks.text: Fed could pivot dovish faster than taper pace implies if labor stalling turns into genuine loosening.
Term premium call ignores balance-sheet and FCI gap flagged by panel; flight-to-quality could offset taper pressure.
Yields already near 12-month highs (range position 0.90), leaving less room for further upside surprise.

trade.rationale: Maintain modest long-duration-yield-up tilt: inflation still hot and fresh, term premium corroborates via taper-driven long-end supply pressure, but trim size given labor softening and FCI flatness versus prior meeting.

user_prompt:
Your previous meeting
  You called: balance_sheet +0.35, financial_conditions +0.00, inflation +0.62, labor_tightness +0.10, term_premium +0.40
  The position you are carrying: DGS10 +0.60, DGS2 +0.40 (conviction 0.50).
  You said this read would flip if: A sub-0.3% CPI mom print with core PCE y/y turning down, or unemployment breaking above 4.20%/Sahm-gap >0.50pp, would flip this toward flat/down.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print confirms continued balance-sheet expansion: total assets rose to 8,928,129 from 8,911,033, a fresh 52-week high, consistent with my prior "up" call, which was correct. The 4-week change (+67,644) and 13-week change (+246,358, +2.84%) both remain positive, though the pace is clearly decelerating — the taper is visibly working, with monthly purchase caps stepping down from $80bn+ combined to just $30bn combined (Treasuries $20bn + MBS $10bn) starting [date] down from $40bn/$20bn the prior month. This is still net QE, just at a shrinking rate. With purchases still positive but being halved again, assets should keep rising into month-end, but by a smaller increment than the trailing 4-week pace suggests, since the new lower monthly pace only partially reflects in the data shown. No language yet signals a move to zero purchases or runoff, so I expect continued (slowing) growth in assets, not a reversal. Reserve creation continues, just at a decelerating clip, which is the "why" behind the still-positive but flattening curve of increases.
Would change its mind if: A month-over-month decline in fed_assets, or language announcing an end to purchases/shift to runoff, would flip this to flat or down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: slope fell from 0.42 to 0.39, continuing the flattening trend. The 2s10s spread sits at 0.39pp, essentially at its 12-month low (0.38) with range_position of just 0.01 — meaning the curve is near the flattest point of the past year. All momentum measures confirm this: 21d change -0.24, 63d change -0.59, 252d change -0.91, all negative and accelerating over longer windows. The policy language has shifted decisively hawkish — from tapering to an explicit signal that a hike is imminent ("expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range"), with asset purchases ending in early [date] . This front-loads tightening expectations, pressuring the front end (2Y) higher relative to the long end, which is the classic mechanism for flattening. The 10s30s slope at 0.34 shows the long end itself is not steepening either, consistent with a broad-based flattening rather than a front-end-only phenomenon. With the spread already pinned near cycle lows and hawkish rhetoric intensifying rather than stabilizing, there is no evidence of a rebound — my stated falsifier (sustained readings above 0.70, or 21d turning positive) has not been met. I expect continued modest flattening or stabilization at a compressed level.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.55 sustained for 2+ readings, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has moved from -0.55 to -0.47 over the last several weeks, a clear and steady rise. The 4-week change (+0.09) and 13-week change (+0.10) are consistent with each other, indicating this is not noise but a sustained trend of tightening momentum. The index is now sitting at its 52-week high, with range_position at 1.00 - the top of its recent band - confirming this is the tightest reading in the observed window, even though the level (-0.47) remains below the zero average, so conditions are still net accommodative in absolute terms. My prior flat call was wrong: at that point the climb toward -0.50 was already underway and has now cleared through that threshold, which I had flagged as my trigger to reverse. The steady, monotonic [date] upward over the last several observations (particularly the last 4: -0.54, -0.52, -0.49, -0.47) shows accelerating tightening momentum rather than stabilization. Policy language remains static and accommodative-oriented, but the measurement itself is the graded signal and it points unambiguously higher. I now call this up with good conviction given the consistency across both short and medium windows.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.52 or flattening over 2+ weeks would suggest the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high, nfci

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been invalidated: breakeven_10y broke decisively above 2.50 and has continued climbing to 2.62, the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.77, only 0.14pp below the 12m high of 2.76). The last two readings (2.54→2.62) show acceleration, not consolidation, and the 21-day change (+0.22pp) is now much larger than the 63-day change (+0.07pp), indicating the move is recent and gaining pace rather than fading. The 252-day change (+0.47pp) confirms a sustained uptrend over the year, not noise. Distance from the 2% objective is now +0.62pp, well above where it sat a few observations ago, suggesting market-implied inflation expectations are becoming increasingly unanchored to the upside relative to target. With price action pushing to fresh highs within the range and momentum building rather than decelerating, the evidence supports continuation over reversion. I was wrong to stay flat last time — the multi-observation break above 2.50 that I set as my falsifier has now occurred emphatically. I'll shift to a clear "up" call, tempered only by the fact that breakevens are near the top of their historical range and could see some mean-reversion resistance there.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below 2.50 sustained over 2-3 observations, or a flattening of the 21-day change toward zero, would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.68
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI rose from 7.56% to 7.94% y/y, and every underlying measure confirms continued acceleration rather than any turn. Core PCE climbed again to 5.36% from 5.21%, still rising monotonically for 13 straight readings with no sign of plateauing. The 3m annualized rates for both headline (8.25%) and core (6.65%) remain well above the trailing y/y rates, meaning recent momentum is running hotter than the reported year-over-year figure — a strong signal that the y/y print itself has further to climb before base effects catch up. The outgoing MoM print (0.52%) is below the 3-month average (0.66%), but at 0.52% it's still a very hot monthly pace in absolute terms, nowhere near the sub-0.3% threshold that would have flagged deceleration. Policy language shifted meaningfully: the Committee moved from "inflation having exceeded 2 percent for some time" tapering language to explicitly "inflation well above 2 percent" with tightening now imminent — confirming officials see continued overheating, not moderation. Core-headline gap remains wide but core is still accelerating, which is the more persistent signal. All evidence — momentum, level, and policy characterization — points the same direction with no offsetting signal.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the trailing y/y rate.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 3.90% from 4.00%, matching the 12-month low and putting the unemployment rate at the bottom of its 13-month range (range position 0.00). The Sahm gap is essentially nil (+0.03pp), confirming no loosening signal whatsoever. The 3m change (-0.20pp) and 6m change (-1.20pp) both show continued tightening, not stabilization. My prior "flat" call was proven wrong by this data — the market kept tightening rather than stalling near the natural rate. The level is now 0.10pp below my assumed natural rate anchor of 4%, indicating a historically tight labor market with no sign of turning. Policy language also confirms this: statements shifted from acknowledging "further improvement in the labor market" to declaring a "strong labor market" as justification for raising rates soon — language consistent with a labor market at or beyond full employment, not one showing cracks. Momentum has been consistently downward (declining unemployment) for months. Absent a clear reversal signal, the most likely near-term outcome is continued flatness at this very low level, with a modest risk of a further tick down given the persistent trend, rather than any reversal upward.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.10% or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.30pp would signal a genuine turn toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print shows a sharp drop to 1.83 from 1.97, a notable one-observation decline after several months of gradual increases (63d +0.35pp, 252d +0.39pp). My prior "up" call was directionally right on the medium-term trend but the most recent data point is a pullback, not a continuation. Still, 21d change is only +0.02pp — essentially flat over the past month — so the single-day drop hasn't yet produced sustained negative momentum. The yield sits at 0.74 of its 12-month range (well above the low), still much closer to the top than the bottom, and the 10s30s slope remains a healthy +0.34pp, consistent with ongoing term-premium richness rather than collapse. Policy language continues to taper asset purchases (further $20bn/$10bn reduction beginning in the new month), which reduces official-sector demand for long-end paper and structurally supports higher term premium over time, even though the latest print dipped. Given the tapering continues and the level remains high in its range, I lean toward the drift resuming upward over the next month, but the recent dip and flat 21d change temper confidence. This is a lean, not a strong call.

  You called: balance_sheet +0.30, financial_conditions +0.45, inflation +0.68, labor_tightness -0.20, term_premium +0.15
  The position you are carrying: DGS10 +0.60, DGS2 +0.40 (conviction 0.55).
  You said this read would flip if: A sub-0.3% CPI MoM with core PCE y/y turning down, or unemployment breaking above 4.20%/Sahm-gap >0.50pp, would flip this toward flat/down.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (8,962,474) extends the uptrend: assets rose over the last 4 weeks (+34,345) and 13 weeks (+171,979, +1.96%), and the series sits at its 52-week high with zero drawdown from that high. My prior "up" call was correct — the balance sheet kept expanding through this release. However, the policy language has now shifted materially: the "at least $20bn/$10bn per month" purchase language has been struck, and new language explicitly states the Committee "expects to begin reducing its holdings... at a coming meeting." This is forward guidance for imminent runoff, not yet realized in the data. The measurement series still reflects the tail end of QE (purchases still occurring or just concluding), so the backward-looking pace remains positive. But the "why" has flipped decisively toward tightening, meaning the next month is a transition point — purchases likely stop or reverse before month-end. Given the explicit announcement of a coming shift to balance-sheet reduction, I now expect the growth trajectory to stall or turn flat/down within the next month, even though the most recent hard data still shows expansion. This is a case where language leads the measurement.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent statement announcing a specific runoff cap and start date, or a monthly fed_assets decline, would confirm the shift to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: the spread continued collapsing from 0.11 to 0.06 to 0.04 to 0.04 over the last four readings, sitting right at the 12-month low with slope_range_position at 0.00. The pace of flattening has decelerated in the very last two prints (0.04→0.04, essentially flat), but the broader momentum (21d -0.32, 63d -0.75, 252d -1.54) remains sharply negative, and the curve is now within 4bp of outright inversion (slope_distance_from_flat +0.04pp). Policy language shows the Committee has pivoted to active tightening — raising rates and signaling "ongoing increases" with one dissent favoring an even larger hike — which is a classic driver of front-end-led flattening as the market prices a more aggressive hiking path against a stickier long end. This suggests the flattening pressure is more likely to persist or even push into inversion than to reverse. However, having already fallen to the 12-month low with almost no room left before inversion, the rate of further flattening could slow mechanically, and 10s30s at 0.12 remains a still-positive long-end cushion. On balance I lean toward continued flattening but at reduced conviction versus last time given the two flat readings suggest deceleration.
Would change its mind if: Two+ consecutive readings back above 0.15pp, or slope_change_21d turning positive, would signal flattening has stalled.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has moved from -0.55 to -0.38 over 13 weeks, a steady, monotonic climb toward zero with no reversals in the last 13 observations. The 4-week change (+0.09) is actually larger in slope than the 13-week average pace, indicating the tightening trend is not stalling but if anything modestly accelerating. Range position sits at 1.00 (52-week high), confirming this is the tightest reading in the lookback window, though the index remains well below zero (-0.38), so conditions are still historically loose in absolute terms. My prior call was "up" with 0.65 conviction and the falsifier (reversal below -0.52 or 2+ week flattening) did not trigger — the series continued rising smoothly, so that call was correct and the trend evidence still supports continuation. Policy language describes conditions as "accommodative" reflecting asset purchases, but this is boilerplate that lags the measured index and doesn't contradict the measured drift toward tighter (less negative) readings. Given the consistent, unbroken upward momentum across all horizons (4w and 13w both positive, no down-ticks in the trailing observations), I expect nfci to continue rising modestly toward month end, though the absolute distance from zero remains large enough that this is not an extreme move.
Would change its mind if: A reversal below -0.44 or a flat/declining 2-week stretch would suggest the tightening trend has stalled.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_high

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.65) was wrong-footed by the latest data: breakeven_10y peaked at 2.95 and has since fallen three consecutive observations to 2.86, 2.86, 2.84 — a clear reversal from the uptrend that had been in place. The 21d and 63d changes are still positive (+0.13, +0.26 pp) because they're measured from lower base levels several weeks back, but the most recent three prints show a distinct rollover, not a continuation. This matches part of my stated falsifier (flattening/reversal of the recent trend), so I should update. The level remains elevated — 0.84pp above the 2% target and in the top 85% of its 12-month range — so inflation expectations are still historically high, but the near-term momentum has clearly turned down. With three straight declines and no sign yet of stabilization, the month-ahead path looks more likely to continue drifting lower or flatten than to resume the prior ascent. Conviction is moderate rather than high because the multi-month averages (63d, 252d) still show a net rise, and three observations is a short reversal to hang a strong call on.
Would change its mind if: A bounce back above 2.90 sustained for 2+ observations would indicate the reversal was noise and the uptrend has resumed.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.75
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct and the evidence still supports it strongly. Headline CPI y/y rose again to 8.57% from 7.94%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.60% from 5.36% — both accelerating, not decelerating. Momentum is the key signal here: the outgoing MoM print jumped to 1.12%, well above the 0.80% trailing 3-month average, and headline 3m annualized (10.09%) is running far above the current y/y rate (8.57%), meaning the y/y rate has further to rise before it could plateau. Core 3m annualized (6.27%) also sits above core y/y (5.60%), confirming persistent underlying pressure rather than a peak. The yoy_range_position of 1.00 confirms we are at a fresh 12-month high with no sign of deceleration. None of my falsifier conditions were met — MoM did not fall below 0.3%, core PCE did not turn down, and 3m annualized did not fall below trailing y/y. Policy language shifted from patient/accommodative framing to explicit acknowledgment that inflation is "well above 2 percent," reinforcing that this is now viewed as a serious, broadening problem rather than transitory. All signals point to continued acceleration into the next release.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print below 0.3% combined with core PCE y/y turning down and 3m annualized headline falling below the trailing y/y rate.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate fell again to 3.70% from 3.90%, extending a clear downtrend: -0.20pp over 3 months, -1.00pp over 6 months, and -2.40pp over 12 months. The rate sits at its 12-month low, with unrate_above_12m_low at 0.00pp and range_position at 0.00, meaning there is no distance-off-lows signal to worry about — the opposite of a Sahm-rule trigger. The Sahm gap of 0.17pp is well below any concerning threshold. My prior "flat" call was too cautious; the print came in at 3.70%, below the 4.10% level I said would falsify flat toward loosening, and instead the market tightened further. The rate is now 0.30pp below my assumed natural rate of 4%, indicating a labor market running tight rather than loosening. Policy language confirms this: "strong labor market," "indicators of economic activity and employment have continued to strengthen," and reduced asset purchases in light of labor market improvement — all consistent with continued tightening, not the beginning of a turn. With no evidence of momentum reversal, deceleration, or claims data suggesting a inflection, I lean toward continued tightness. A single month at a fresh cyclical low is not a guarantee of further decline, but there is no basis to expect a reversal either — flat-to-down is the best supported call.
Would change its mind if: A print above roughly 3.90-4.00% (reversing the recent low) or a Sahm-gap rising above 0.30pp would signal a genuine turn toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was directionally correct: yields continued climbing through the recent window, though the last two prints (2.35, 2.32) show a modest pullback from the cycle high of 2.48. Level remains near the top of the 12-month range (range_position 0.88), well above the 12m low, confirming an entrenched uptrend rather than a reversal. Momentum is still strongly positive at 21d/63d/252d horizons (+0.46/+0.80/+0.58pp), even if the very latest two ticks have eased slightly. The 30s10s slope at +0.12pp is modestly positive and not flattening aggressively, so no clear signal of trend exhaustion yet. Policy language shift toward balance-sheet runoff ("expects to begin reducing its holdings of Treasury securities...") is a fresh hawkish-for-duration development that should, if anything, add upward pressure on long-end yields via reduced official-sector demand — reinforcing rather than reversing the term-premium uptrend. The two-session dip is too small and too recent to call a reversal per my stated falsifier (multi-session move below 1.75 with negative momentum and flattening slope), which is nowhere close to being met. I maintain the same call, with slightly higher conviction given the balance-sheet runoff language is a new, additive force on top of already-elevated levels and range position.
Would change its mind if: A confirmed multi-session move below ~2.10 with negative 21d momentum and a flattening/inverting 30s10s slope would signal the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: The panel still leans hawkish on net, but the composition has shifted since last meeting in ways that matter for level. Inflation remains the strongest, freshest, highest-conviction driver: headline and core accelerating to fresh highs, 3m annualized running above y/y, no falsifier hit — this is the dominant near-term repricing force and argues for higher yields. Labor tightness also firmed (unemployment down to 3.70%, fresh 12m low, Sahm gap low) — a tighter labor market is disinflationary-resistant and adds to the "Fed has room/cause to tighten" case, reinforcing upward yield pressure, even though its own conviction (0.35) is modest. Term premium confirms continued upward drift in long yields, now with an explicit fresh catalyst (balance-sheet runoff language) layered on top — this is a genuine repricing input, not stale, and it corroborates rather than contradicts inflation's read. Balance sheet itself is more ambiguous: backward data still shows expansion, but the analyst flags an explicit, forward-looking pivot to runoff — which pushes yields up (less demand) even though the driver's own headline (assets) will flatten/fall. That's consistent with, not contrary to, the term premium's up call, and closes the gap the term-premium analyst flagged. Financial conditions continue tightening steadily (NFCI up), a slow-moving but corroborating signal. Overall, three of my most decision-relevant, non-stale inputs (inflation, labor tightness, term premium) all point the same direction — up — with balance-sheet's forward guidance reinforcing via reduced duration demand. Nothing here contradicts my prior read; if anything the balance-sheet pivot and continued inflation acceleration strengthen the case. I hold the position, keeping duration short-biased on the long end where term premium and reduced official demand most directly operate, but I do trim size modestly given the curve-flattening dynamic is a separate seat and my carry is now more concentrated in the same three confirming drivers rather than diversified across five.

risks.text: Balance-sheet runoff timing is still guidance, not realized; delay would remove one upward force on long-end yields.
Term premium's last two prints ticked down; a further pullback could signal exhaustion of the long-end uptrend.
Curve pod's flattening call implies front-end-led moves that could compress the level trade if inversion accelerates.
A single soft CPI/PCE print could quickly flip inflation's currently dominant, high-conviction call.

trade.rationale: Inflation acceleration, tightening labor market, and reinforced term-premium/balance-sheet runoff all point to higher yields; trim size slightly given curve-flattening risk and financial conditions being largely priced.

why.inflation: Headline and core both re-accelerated to fresh 12m highs, 3m annualized above y/y, no falsifier hit; strongest live driver.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment fell to 3.70%, fresh 12m low, Sahm gap low — labor market's own measure (unemployment) falls, meaning tighter market.
why.term_premium: 10y yields near cycle highs, momentum positive across horizons, now reinforced by fresh balance-sheet runoff guidance — upgraded from prior.
why.balance_sheet: Backward data still expanding but explicit runoff guidance signals imminent flattening/decline in holdings — forward-looking, not yet in data.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI climbing steadily toward zero, accelerating slightly; slow but corroborating tightening signal, kept but down-weighted as well-anticipated.

arbitrated.notes:
Net read: yields still biased up, but with less conviction than last meeting and I'm trimming size rather than reversing. The two genuinely rate-moving drivers — inflation and labor — both point to a flattening-out rather than a fresh acceleration. Inflation: headline y/y just posted its first downtick in 13 months and MoM decelerated sharply to 0.31% from a 0.71% trailing average — that's a real softening signal, though core PCE is still creeping at cycle highs so underlying persistence hasn't broken. This only partially confirms my prior falsifier (sub-0.3% MoM), so I move inflation from clearly-up to modestly-down rather than flipping hard. Labor: unemployment held flat at 3.70%, the 12-month low, with Sahm gap negligible — no further tightening, but no loosening either. This is a genuine change from my prior "further tightening" lean (-0.35) to flat; the market has plateaued at a tight level rather than continuing to tighten, which removes some upward pressure on yields from that channel.

The slower, largely-priced drivers still lean up: term premium is at fresh 12-month highs with strong multi-horizon momentum and now a credible balance-sheet-runoff signal in the statement — this is the most rate-relevant driver still firing, even though it's my own residual synthesis. Financial conditions are still drifting toward tightening but decelerating, and balance sheet is genuinely flat/plateauing (purchases ended, runoff not yet active) — a wash. Inflation expectations analyst (I don't own, but note the cross-read) shows breakevens near range highs, in tension with the inflation analyst's softening headline — a real disagreement that the inflation analyst flags as an unaddressed gap (lacks inflation_expectations input). That gap plus the labor plateau argues for trimming, not abandoning, the long-duration-short bet.

Net: keep a modest bear-steepish level tilt but reduce size given inflation and labor have both turned toward stabilization/softening versus last meeting's clear tightening picture.

Balance_sheet flipped to "up" (4-week bounce) but analyst flags this as likely noise against a still-negative 13-week trend; I treat this as low-value/stale for now.

arbitrated.notes:
Since the prior meeting, the panel has shifted meaningfully but not uniformly, and the net vector is still modestly up on the level though with reduced conviction. Term premium — the single most trade-relevant driver for yield level, since it directly reflects the 10Y's own price action and is live, momentum-confirmed data — flipped from down to up with rising conviction (0.55), citing sustained 21d/63d upward momentum, elevated range position, and ongoing balance-sheet runoff mechanically adding duration supply. That is a genuine, fresh signal and the core reason to keep a duration-up tilt. Balance sheet stays in confirmed runoff (down, 0.55), which is slow-moving and mostly priced, but it reinforces the term-premium logic via the supply channel — the two are not independent, and term_premium's own reported gap (lacking balance_sheet detail) means I should lean on balance_sheet's confirmation rather than double-count it.

Against that, the near-term repricing drivers have both turned dovish for yields: inflation (CPI and breakevens both) is now down with 0.50-0.55 conviction, a reversal from the prior up call, driven by two soft MoM prints and a breakdown in 10Y breakevens below 2.50. Labor tightness remains flat/tight (0.30, weak), a stale-ish read with no real move. Financial conditions is flat with low conviction (0.25), having been wrong last time — essentially no signal.

Given inflation and breakevens (the fastest-moving, most repricing-relevant drivers) are now clearly pulling down, while term premium (also live, and arguably captures duration-supply momentum directly) pulls up, this is a genuinely split panel on the rate axis. That argues for shrinking the position rather than reversing it — the previous up-tilt is still defensible via term premium/supply, but the offsetting inflation reversal is a real signal I can't ignore. I trim conviction and size but keep the directional lean, since no driver has flipped decisively against duration-up on its own high-value axis.

Policy language reinforces this: "job gains have been robust... unemployment rate has remained low," with the added "Nonetheless" suggesting the Committee is calling out labor strength as notable or perhaps in tension with other conditions, but not describing any loosening emerging in labor markets themselves. Given the rate is at a fresh low with no upward momentum, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-tight next month, expecting the unemployment rate to hold near 3.5% or tick marginally within noise. There is no evidence here of turning higher.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a turning point toward loosening; a print at or below 3.50% would suggest tightening is still active.

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct in direction: dgs10 rose again to 3.83, extending the trend from 3.49 ten observations ago. However, the most recent print shows the 10y pulled back from a 3.97 peak to 3.72-3.83, so momentum has moderated even though the level remains near the top of its range. Range position at 0.95 confirms the yield is sitting near its 12-month high, and the 63d/252d changes (+0.95pp, +2.29pp) show a strong, persistent uptrend. The 30s10s slope is only -0.04pp, essentially flat, which doesn't show the kind of curve inversion at the long end that would flag an exhaustion of the term-premium rise. Continued balance-sheet runoff language remains a structural tailwind for higher long-end yields via increased net supply absorption. That said, the pullback from 3.97 to 3.72 before the latest tick back up to 3.83 introduces some two-sided risk — the move is decelerating even if not reversing. I'll maintain an "up" call but temper conviction given the loss of momentum near cycle highs and the risk of consolidation at these elevated levels.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward 3.50 with negative 21d momentum and a flattening/inverting 30s10s would signal the upward push has genuinely stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Panel largely reaffirms last meeting's picture rather than overturning it, but with important nuances. Core inflation reaccelerated (core PCE 5.36 vs 5.11) even as headline stalled — this is the most trade-relevant, live signal since inflation/labor data reprice the front end fastest. Labor market stayed tight (unemployment at 3.50%, 12m low, no loosening evidence), reinforcing a still-tight labor read though conviction is modest and mostly confirmatory of prior. Term premium call stays "up" but the analyst itself flags moderating momentum (pullback from 3.97 peak) — I read this as a live but weakening signal, still net supportive of higher long-end yields given persistent QT-driven supply, but less forceful than last meeting. Balance sheet continues its clean monotonic runoff (down again, -0.55→-0.65 conviction), a slow, well-anticipated drag that's largely priced — I keep it as a structural tailwind for higher yields but downweight its marginal informational content. Financial conditions tightened further (NFCI at 52w high, unambiguous uptrend) — this is a genuinely fresh, non-stale read and its tightening bias (higher NFCI, tighter conditions) is more consistent with upward yield pressure via Fed staying restrictive, a reversal from my prior "no view" stance.

Net: inflation reacceleration (core) + persistent labor tightness + continuing QT + fresh financial-conditions tightening all point the same direction — modestly higher yields — even though term premium's own momentum is cooling. No driver has flipped toward easier/lower yields; breakevens falling is real but that's the inflation-expectations pod's turf, not a level override here. I maintain the existing long-duration-yields (short bonds) tilt, unchanged in size given the panel is confirmatory, not newly convicted.

Breakevens at 12m lows suggest market already pricing disinflation; a rebound there could contradict the inflation reacceleration read.

trade.rationale: Panel confirms rather than reverses prior read: tightening financial conditions, core inflation reacceleration, persistent labor tightness, and continued QT all lean toward modestly higher yields, offset by term premium's cooling momentum. Hold existing modest long-yield (short-duration) tilt unchanged.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.72
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

Report:

The most recent print (8,723,090) is well below the 13w average (8,810,278) and near fresh lows relative to the 52w high (-242,397 below high). The 4-week change is -72,477M and 13-week change is -166,914M, both showing continued, fairly steady monthly-cap-paced decline with no sign of deceleration or reversal — in fact the last three readings (8,758,969 / 8,743,922 / 8,723,090) show consistent drawdown of roughly $15-20B per week, consistent with ongoing QT. Policy language is unchanged, explicitly confirming the Committee continues reducing Treasury and MBS holdings per the balance-sheet reduction plan, with no mention of pausing or slowing caps. There is no evidence of a taper in pace or any statement change. Given the consistency of the weekly declines and unchanged runoff language, I expect total assets to be lower again at month-end, continuing the established trend.
Would change its mind if: Fed_assets rising above the 13w average for multiple consecutive weeks, or statement language pausing/ending balance-sheet runoff.

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has re-inverted after a brief bounce: from -0.28 up to -0.25 (least inverted point), then rolling back down to -0.41 at the latest print, matching levels seen 10 observations ago. My prior "flat" call was too sanguine given the last two prints show renewed flattening (-0.39, -0.41), not stabilization. slope_change_21d is roughly flat (-0.01pp) but slope_change_63d is -0.11pp, confirming a medium-term flattening trend reasserting itself. The spread sits at just 0.06 of its 12-month range (near the 12-month low of -0.51), a deeply inverted position with essentially no cushion. The Fed's hiking language intensified (funds rate raised further to 3-3.25% with "ongoing increases" anticipated), which historically pressures the front end (2Y) more than the long end, mechanically compressing or re-inverting the curve. There's no sign of a durable reversal — the brief steepening move stalled and reversed. Given the falsifier I set previously (sustained break above -0.15 with persistent positive 21d change) has clearly not been met — the opposite occurred — I'm shifting toward a mild-to-moderate down (further flattening/re-inversion) call, though the 21d change being near-zero tempers conviction that this continues aggressively next month.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.25pp with slope_change_21d turning and holding positive (>0.10pp) for consecutive readings would signal renewed steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_63d, slope_change_21d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is validated but momentum is now stalling. NFCI rose steadily from -0.24 to -0.10 over roughly 10 weeks (a clear tightening trend, still visible in the 13-week change of +0.09), but the last three prints are -0.10, -0.10, -0.11 — essentially flat with a tiny tick down. The 4-week change has collapsed to +0.01, confirming the trend has lost steam right at the top of its 52-week range (range_position 0.98, at the 52w high of -0.10). This is a classic deceleration/plateau pattern after a sustained easing-conditions unwind. The index remains below zero (distance_from_zero -0.11), so conditions are still slightly looser than historical average even after the tightening move. With momentum flattening and the latest tick actually negative, I don't see strong evidence for continued tightening into next month; more likely a pause or mild give-back near this multi-period high. I'm downgrading conviction on "up" substantially and leaning toward flat, with a slight tilt that a reversal (easing) is at least as plausible as further tightening given the plateau at the range ceiling.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above -0.08 with 4-week change reaccelerating past +0.03 would revive the tightening call; a drop back below -0.15 confirms reversal to easing.

The series shows a choppy but net-positive path: 21d change +0.33pp, though 63d is flat (+0.01pp) and 252d is still negative (-0.13pp), indicating the longer-term decline has stalled and reversed in the near term rather than continuing. The last two readings (2.51, 2.52) sit at the high end of recent oscillation, matching the prior peak of 2.59 seen a few observations back, suggesting expectations have stabilized in a 2.44-2.59 range with an upward bias in the most recent month. Range position (0.43) is mid-range historically, and distance from the 2% target (+0.52pp) remains elevated but not extreme. Given the clear reversal of my prior down call and the recent 21d momentum, I now lean toward continued firmness or modest further increase over the next month, though the flat 63d trend tempers conviction. This is a lean toward up, not a strong conviction call given the choppiness.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call is roughly vindicated: headline y/y fell from 8.19 to 7.76 (-0.43pp), a meaningful decline, while the mom_gap condition I set (headline MoM below 0.2%, core PCE under 5.2%) was NOT met — outgoing MoM was 0.56% and core PCE actually ticked up to 5.61 from 5.36, its highest in the sample. So the disinflation case did not clearly restore, but headline still moved down more than I expected given a sticky core.
  
  Looking at momentum: headline_3m_annualized (4.26%) is now running below the 12m trailing y/y (7.76%), consistent with further y/y deceleration mechanically as high base months roll off. But the latest MoM print (0.56%) reaccelerated from the two prior very-low prints (0.06, 0.42), and core PCE y/y is rising again, its third straight increase from 5.11 to 5.36 to 5.61 — this is the most hawkish core signal in the whole series and argues persistence, not disintegration, of underlying price pressure. Headline's y/y decline is being driven by base effects and volatile components rather than genuine disinflation, since core is diverging upward. Given the tension — headline trending down, core accelerating — I lean toward the base-effect arithmetic continuing to pull headline y/y down modestly next print, but with reduced conviction given core's inflection upward signals underlying stickiness that could stall further headline declines.
Would change its mind if: A rise in headline MoM above 0.8% or core PCE y/y continuing above 5.6% would signal renewed broad-based acceleration, invalidating the down call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.60% came in within my prior stated flat-band (below the 3.70% loosening trigger, above the 3.50% tightening trigger), so my previous "flat" call was directionally correct. The series shows unemployment bouncing narrowly between 3.50 and 3.60 for the last five readings, well off the 12-month high of 4.10 and near the 12-month low of 3.50. The 3-month change is a modest +0.10pp, 6-month is -0.10pp, and 12-month is -0.90pp — essentially no consistent trend, just noise around a tight labor market. The Sahm-rule style gap (unrate vs 3m-avg minus 12m-low) is only +0.07pp, far below the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal recession-onset loosening. Distance from a 4% natural rate is -0.40pp, meaning the market is still tighter than normal. Policy language softened slightly (dropped "nonetheless") but still affirms robust job gains and a low unemployment rate, consistent with continued tightness rather than any turning point. I see no rapid-rise signal and no level breakout. I'll maintain a flat call, tilting my next trigger levels tighter given the two-sided bounce pattern already observed at 3.60%.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a turning point toward loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

arbitrated.notes:
The panel largely reaffirms last meeting's read with some softening at the edges. Balance sheet runoff continues steadily (down, high conviction, fresh) — this is a slow, well-anticipated driver but its persistence still supports term-premium/duration pressure on the long end, and it directly informs term_premium's own call (a named gap the term_premium analyst lacked). Term premium itself reports "up" but with reduced conviction (0.35 vs prior 0.40) — 10Y is choppy, bounced off a pullback, still near cycle highs. Financial conditions has stalled to flat/plateau after a tightening run — this reduces the tightening tailwind I leaned on previously, a genuine change I should register. Inflation is the most relevant near-term repricing driver: headline y/y still falling on base effects, but core PCE has now risen three straight prints to a fresh high (5.61), the most hawkish signal in the series. That's a live, active driver and it argues against declaring victory on disinflation — it keeps upward pressure on yields via sticky core, even though headline optics improve. Labor tightness remains flat/rangebound, stale-ish but consistent — no repricing signal either way. Inflation_expectations (breakeven) confirms an upside reversal, corroborating the core PCE stickiness rather than the headline decline — I weight this cross-read as meaningfully supportive of the "up" case since breakevens are a live, front-line market read.

Net: the composite level case is still tilted up, but conviction should soften slightly versus last meeting since financial conditions plateaued and term premium's own conviction dropped. The core inflation acceleration and breakeven reversal are the most decision-relevant new information, keeping me long duration exposure to higher yields rather than flipping. No named falsifier from last meeting was hit — 10Y did not close below 3.05, core PCE rose rather than fell under 5.2%. I hold the position, trimming conviction modestly.

Inflation: headline CPI y/y continues to decelerate (7.76→7.12), largely base-effect driven, while core PCE stays sticky in the low-to-mid 5% range — net a mild-down/decelerating call, but not a collapse. Term premium: 10Y pulled back from 3.83 to 3.68 (21d momentum -0.34) but the 63d/252d trend and range position (0.80) still sit elevated; the analyst downgraded from "up" to a hedged "flat," which I read as a genuine loss of upward conviction rather than a reversal. Balance sheet: QT continues unambiguously (assets -101.7bn 4wk, -230bn 13wk), which is a slow, well-anticipated, already-priced drag that pushes yields up mechanically but carries little fresh repricing power. Net: the near-term repricing drivers (labor flat, inflation cooling on headline but core still sticky, financial conditions now easing) are collectively pulling the level view down from where I sat last month, while term premium's own conviction has weakened rather than strengthened. This is not a clean reversal signal — my flip trigger (10Y below 3.05, core PCE under 5.2%) hasn't fired — but the composite has moved enough that I should trim my long-yield tilt rather than hold it unchanged or add to it.

Given the persistent tightness, low volatility, and no emerging trend, I expect the unemployment rate to remain essentially flat at the next report, likely printing in the 3.50-3.60% band. There is no evidence of acceleration in either direction that would justify a stronger directional call.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: the 10-year continued rising rather than stabilizing, moving from ~3.67-3.69 up to 3.88, with 21d momentum +0.20pp and 63d +0.16pp both positive, confirming a renewed uptrend rather than consolidation. The series shows a clear grind higher over the last several prints (3.68→3.75→3.84→3.88, a brief dip to 3.83, then back to 3.88), sitting at 0.86 of its 12-month range — near the top, well above the 12m low by 2.36pp. The 30s10s slope is positive (+0.09pp) and not flattening, meaning the long end continues to cheapen relative to the belly, consistent with ongoing term-premium expansion rather than exhaustion. Continued balance-sheet runoff per the policy language reinforces upward pressure on long-end yields via reduced official-sector demand. There is no evidence here of flight-to-quality or reversal — momentum, level, and range position all point the same direction. I am revising toward an "up" call given the two consecutive months of higher yields with supportive slope and runoff context, though conviction is moderate given the pause/dip at 3.83 before the latest print suggests some choppiness rather than a clean breakout.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below 3.75 with negative 21d momentum and a flattening 30s10s slope would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting the panel has shifted in a direction that argues for less duration conviction, not more. Term premium flipped from flat to up (10Y grinding to 3.88, near top of its 12m range, positive 30s10s slope, no reversal signal) — this is a live, fresh read and it directly repositions the long end, which is exactly the kind of driver I should weight heavily on the rate axis. Balance sheet continues its clear, confirmed runoff (down call, high conviction) — mechanically this removes official-sector duration demand and is consistent with upward pressure on long yields, reinforcing term premium's read rather than offsetting it. Financial conditions continues easing (NFCI grinding to -0.20, no reversal) — looser conditions reduce the case for a flight-to-quality bid and are broadly consistent with higher, not lower, yields, though this driver is slower-moving and largely priced. Inflation (headline) confirms deceleration (6.40% y/y, soft MoM) but core PCE remains sticky at 5.21% with elevated 3m annualized — this is a genuine two-sided signal: headline disinflation argues for lower yields, sticky core argues against a clean down call. Labor stays flat/tight (3.50% unemployment, no momentum) — this is a stale-ish, no-view driver for the level axis; tight labor keeps a floor under yields but adds no fresh directional information this month.

Net: the fresher, market-moving drivers (term premium, balance sheet, financial conditions) all point the same way — modestly higher yields — while the slower/mixed drivers (inflation, labor) don't provide enough to overturn that. This confirms rather than contradicts my prior long-duration-light stance; the my stated flip conditions (10Y sub-3.05, core PCE sub-5.2%) have not been met — core PCE is still 5.21%, right at the edge, and 10Y is nowhere near 3.05. I hold the existing modest short-duration-equivalent (long yield) tilt with unchanged sizing given the split between headline disinflation and everything else pointing up.

Your previous meeting
  You called: balance_sheet -0.75, financial_conditions -0.60, inflation -0.35, labor_tightness +0.00, term_premium +0.60
  The position you are carrying: DGS10 +0.12, DGS2 +0.06 (conviction 0.20).

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: fed_assets fell every week in the series, from 8,489,039 to 8,470,557, continuing a steady runoff. The 4-week change (-80,612) and 13-week change (-252,533) both confirm an ongoing, fairly consistent pace of balance-sheet reduction, with assets now 494,930 below the 52-week high and well under the 13w average (8,572,667.85), consistent with sustained QT rather than a plateau. The policy language explicitly reaffirms continued runoff of Treasury and agency MBS holdings per the announced plan, with no mention of tapering the cap or pausing reinvestment. There is no evidence of deceleration in the weekly decrements — the last several weekly drops (508,587→489,039→470,557) show pace roughly $18-19B/week, consistent with prior weeks. Nothing here suggests a reversal or plateau is imminent for next month-end. Absent any statement change signaling a slowdown or halt, the mechanical, policy-conforming runoff should continue at a similar pace into the next observation month-end. I maintain a confident "down" call, slightly below previous conviction only because the pace seems slightly more volatile week-to-week than perfectly linear, but directionally unambiguous.
Would change its mind if: Weekly fed_assets rising above the 13w average for multiple consecutive weeks, or statement language pausing/ending balance-sheet runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at low conviction (0.25) was roughly right in that no clean break occurred, but the evidence actually leaned more flattening than I credited. The last print (-0.69) sits right at the low end of recent range, essentially matching the series minimum seen (-0.70) a couple readings back, and well below the 12-month high of +0.63. slope_range_position of 0.10 confirms the spread is near the bottom decile of its 12-month range. Momentum signals are consistently negative: -0.18pp over 21 days, -0.35pp over 63 days, and a steep -1.41pp over 252 days — all pointing toward further inversion/flattening, not stabilization. The last ten observations oscillate in a narrow band (-0.65 to -0.70) with no clear reversal, but the drift within that band is toward the more negative end (ending at -0.69 after touching -0.65). Policy language shows another rate hike (to 4-1/4–4-1/2%) with continued "ongoing increases" language, which historically pressures front-end yields up relative to long end, consistent with the flattening pressure already evident in dgs2 (4.21%) exceeding dgs10 (3.52%) by the full inverted spread. Given the continued restrictive tilt and multi-horizon negative momentum, I lean toward modest further flattening rather than flat, though the band-bound behavior limits conviction.
Would change its mind if: A move above -0.55pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would signal steepening; a break below -0.80 would confirm accelerating inversion.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, dgs2, dgs10

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues to decline steadily, from -0.12 to -0.29 over the 13-week window, with the most recent print (-0.29) the lowest in the series. The 4-week change (-0.08) and 13-week change (-0.18) are both firmly negative, and momentum shows no sign of deceleration — each successive weekly reading has been lower than the last for the entire visible history. This confirms my prior "down" call: conditions have continued to ease, not stalled or reversed. The index sits well below zero (-0.29), meaning financial conditions are looser than the historical average, and it is also below its own 13-week average (-0.20), reinforcing that the easing trend is still active rather than mean-reverting. The series sits at 0.57 of its 52-week range, roughly mid-range, still with room to fall toward the 52-week low of -0.54, and no sign of exhaustion in the trend. There is no indication in the measurements of a base or reversal forming. Absent a change in the pace of decline, the most reasonable expectation for month-end is a continuation of this easing trajectory, likely landing modestly below -0.29.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above -0.24 with 4-week change turning positive would signal the easing move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong: after peaking at 2.33 the breakeven has now printed two consecutive declines to 2.29 then 2.24, exactly the falsifier condition I set. The 10-year breakeven has round-tripped from 2.33 down to 2.24, a -0.09pp pullback in the last two observations, and the 21d and 63d changes are both negative (-0.03 and -0.21 pp respectively), confirming the recent momentum has turned down rather than resuming the earlier uptrend from 2.12. The series is still up considerably from its 12-month low (be_above_12m_low +0.12pp) and sits only 13% into its 12-month range, but the near-term trajectory is now clearly softening. Distance from the 2% target remains material at +0.24pp, so expectations are still anchored above objective, but the direction of travel over the last several readings is down, not up. Given two consecutive down-prints and negative short/medium-term change measures, I read this as a modest downward lean for the next month rather than a continuation of new highs. Conviction is moderate, not high, since the longer 252d change is smaller in magnitude and the level remains elevated relative to the 12-month low.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.29-2.33 over the next few observations would indicate the pullback was noise, not a new downtrend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was directionally correct: headline y/y fell from 7.12 to 6.33 (-0.79pp), continuing the deceleration trend, and my stated falsifiers (MoM above 0.6% or core PCE back above 5.5%) did not trigger — outgoing MoM was 0.53% and core PCE fell to 4.97%, its lowest in the series. That said, the outgoing MoM of 0.53% is a step up from the prior month's 0.02%, and the 3m annualized rates (headline 3.28%, core 4.08%) show core running hotter than the y/y trend suggests, with core_yoy_change_3m only -0.64pp versus -1.43pp for headline. This gap (core_minus_headline -1.36pp) means headline's recent favorable readings are more energy/base-effect driven, while core PCE, though improving, remains far above target (+2.97pp) and its momentum is less convincingly decelerating. The yoy_range_position at 0.00 confirms headline is at its 12-month low, which is constructive, but the single-month MoM uptick warrants caution against expecting another sharp drop next month. Net: disinflation trend intact but momentum has softened compared to the pace of decline seen in prior months, so I expect continued but slower improvement.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print above 0.6% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.2% would signal stalling/reacceleration and invalidate continued deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate has been essentially flat at cycle lows, printing 3.50% in the most recent observation, matching the trailing 12-month low and sitting well below the ~4% natural rate estimate (distance_from_natural -0.50pp). My prior flat call was correct: the series has oscillated narrowly between 3.50-3.60% for the last six readings with no directional break, and the latest print confirms that range-bound behavior rather than the loosening or renewed-tightening thresholds I set. The 3m change (-0.10pp) and 6m change (0.00pp) both indicate no meaningful momentum in either direction, and the Sahm gap remains negligible at +0.03pp, far from the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Policy language continues to describe the labor market as robust with low unemployment, consistent with the data. With the rate pinned at its 12-month low and no acceleration in either direction visible in the last several prints, the most defensible call remains flat. There is a slight asymmetry — the level is very tight, and historically such tightness eventually gives way to gradual normalization — but nothing in the recent trend supports calling a turn yet. I maintain flat with slightly higher conviction than before given the confirmed stability.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "up" call at 0.45 conviction was wrong for this window: the 10y has actually drifted down over the last 21d (-0.31pp) and 63d (-0.44pp), even as the level sits at 3.52%, near the recent range but well off the extreme high. The fall satisfies part of my own falsifier (negative 21d momentum), though the level is still above 3.75 was never breached and 30s10s hasn't flattened (it's actually a modestly positive 0.13pp, little changed). Looking at the raw series, yields rose from 3.37 to a peak near 3.55 and have since chopped sideways/down slightly, ending at 3.52 — essentially a plateau with mild softening. The 252d change (+1.67pp) shows the dominant multi-quarter trend remains firmly up, and range position (0.71) confirms we're in the upper part of the 12-month band, consistent with continued balance-sheet runoff pressure on term premium. But the most recent short-term momentum is flat-to-slightly-down, not a continuation of the uptrend. Given the conflicting signals — strong medium-term uptrend vs. recent stalling — I'll moderate toward a flat-to-slightly-up call rather than reasserting a confident up view.
Would change its mind if: A decisive break below 3.40 with negative 21d momentum persisting and a flattening/inverting 30s10s slope would confirm the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  curve_slope lacks financial_conditions — Tightening/loosening in broader conditions often co-moves with curve flattening/steepening pressure.
  curve_slope lacks term_premium — Rising term premium could independently steepen the curve regardless of Fed path.
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI surprises often drive short-term breakeven swings and would clarify if this dip is durable.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — Tightening financial conditions can compress breakevens independent of inflation views, would help disentangle cause.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of ongoing Treasury runoff/issuance supply would clarify whether upward pressure on long end is intensifying or easing.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment shifts could signal flight-to-quality flows that would push yields down independent of supply factors.
