Reconciling against my prior carry (small long-yield tilt, DGS10 +0.12/DGS2 +0.06, conviction 0.20): the panel has moved somewhat against that tilt, but not decisively.

Trade-relevant drivers for the rate axis are inflation and labor_tightness (near-term reaction function) plus term_premium (directly the long-end level). Balance_sheet and financial_conditions are slow-moving/well-anticipated and mostly priced already, so I downweight their confident calls despite high conviction scores.

Inflation: headline CPI still decelerating (7.12→6.33% y/y) and core PCE at series low (4.97%), but momentum has clearly softened (MoM stepped up to 0.53% from 0.02%, core 3m annualized running hotter than y/y). This is a weaker "down" than last meeting — still net disinflationary, but momentum stalling caps how much further yields should fall on this driver alone.

Labor_tightness: flat, pinned at cycle lows (3.50%), no directional break. This is a genuine no-view/flat contribution to yields — tight labor keeps a floor under yields but no fresh repricing signal here.

Term_premium: the "up" call from last meeting was walked back to flat-to-slightly-up. 10Y momentum over 21d/63d is actually negative, though 252d trend and range position still lean elevated. This is the most direct read on the long end and it has genuinely softened versus last meeting — the loud "up" story I leaned on previously is not confirmed today.

Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  The previous down call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 6.40 to 5.96, continuing a clear downtrend, and the falsifier conditions (MoM >0.6% or core PCE back above 5.2%) were not triggered — outgoing MoM was 0.34% and core PCE printed 4.94%, both below thresholds. Momentum signals are mixed but net still favor deceleration: headline_3m_annualized (3.61%) sits well below the latest y/y print (5.96%), implying further downward pull if recent monthly prints persist. Core PCE continues to grind lower (4.94%, a fresh 13-month low) though it remains far above target (+2.94pp) and its pace of decline has slowed (core_yoy_change_3m only -0.52pp vs -1.17pp for headline), so I weight headline's momentum as the more informative signal for the next print while noting core's persistence argues against a sharp deceleration. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is negative, and the 3-month average MoM (0.30%) is modest, consistent with continued but decelerating disinflation. Policy language softened materially ("eased somewhat but remains elevated"), corroborating the data-driven easing narrative. I expect another modest decline in headline CPI y/y at the next release, though the pace of improvement is now slowing versus prior months, so conviction is moderate rather than high.
Would change its mind if: A headline MoM print above 0.5-0.6% or core PCE y/y ticking back up above 5.0% would signal stalling disinflation and invalidate this call.

Call: flat, conviction 0.62
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate has been range-bound between 3.5% and 3.7% for the past 13 months, with the latest print at 3.6%, right in the middle of that range (range_position 0.50) and only 0.10pp above the 12-month low. The 3m and 6m changes are flat at +0.00pp, and the 12m change is actually -0.30pp, indicating no loosening trend at all — if anything a slight net tightening over the year. The Sahm-rule-style gap is a negligible +0.03pp, far below any threshold that would signal recessionary labor-market deterioration. My previous flat call was correct: the print came in at 3.6%, within the established range and not above my 3.70% threshold for confirming loosening. The level itself, at 3.6%, sits meaningfully below the ~4% natural rate estimate (distance -0.40pp), consistent with a still-tight labor market. Policy language continues to describe job gains as robust and unemployment as low, with no shift in tone on labor conditions specifically (the only change was dropping a public-health reference, not a labor-market characterization). With no directional momentum in either the 3m or 6m windows and the print bouncing within a narrow 0.2pp band, there is no basis to call a turn in either direction for the next report.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm loosening; a print at or below 3.40% would suggest renewed tightening beyond the recent range.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting the composition of the panel has shifted meaningfully. Term premium, which I had at +0.10 (weak up-lean), now reports a firmer up call at 0.35: 10y sitting at 0.87 of its 12m range, near cycle highs, with positive 21d/63d momentum and no steepening in 30s10s to signal a reversal. That is the single most trade-relevant driver here since it directly reads the long-end yield level and its own momentum, and it has strengthened, not weakened, since my last call. Inflation (headline CPI, the near-term repricing driver) is still down (-0.45) but is 13 days stale and its own momentum is decelerating (pace of improvement slowing, core PCE decline much slower than headline). A companion breakeven/inflation-expectations report (not one of my named five drivers, but corroborating context) shows breakevens making new highs with positive 21d/63d momentum — this cross-read undercuts the conviction I'd otherwise place on the stale CPI-down call and argues the disinflation trade is losing force. Labor tightness is flat/range-bound with no repricing signal. Balance sheet continues firmly down (-0.78, QT persisting, no taper) — this is a slow, well-anticipated driver already priced into term premium; I keep it as background support for a mild upward yield bias but do not let it drive conviction. Financial conditions have stalled/inflected slightly tighter (-0.29, up from -0.31, momentum flattening) — modest, low-conviction, mostly priced.

Netting these: the fresh, live reads (term premium up, financial conditions inflection, inflation's own deceleration) lean modestly toward higher yields, reinforcing rather than reversing my prior small long-yield tilt. The stale CPI-down call no longer commands as much weight given the breakeven cross-read. I hold the existing modest long-duration/higher-yield position, keeping conviction low given panel disagreement (curve_slope inversion deepening, labor flat) is not itself a level signal.

The statement language still references continued reduction of holdings per previously announced plans, but the actual measurement contradicts that language's implied pace — the "why" text appears stale relative to what's actually happening on the balance sheet. Given the size and recency of the jump, I expect assets to stay elevated or drift only modestly lower next month, not resume the prior runoff trajectory. This is a clear reversal signal, though the single-month spike nature makes me cautious about extrapolating too far.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has shifted meaningfully since last meeting, and the shift is mostly toward lower yields, not higher. Inflation (fresh, 16 days old, high conviction 0.55) confirms continued disinflation with headline CPI down a full point and favorable base effects still working — this is the most trade-relevant driver since it's a live near-term repricing input, and it argues for lower yields. Labor tightness is flat/stale-ish but still shows a tight, non-deteriorating market (3.50%, at 12m low) — this is a mild hawkish offset but doesn't argue for higher yields, just no loosening-driven cut acceleration. Term premium flipped from up to down: the 10y level itself pulled back to 3.48 with negative 21d/63d momentum, directly evidencing lower yields at the level I trade — I weight this heavily since it's the most direct read on the instrument itself. Balance sheet flipped hard from down to up (surge in assets), which is theoretically yield-negative (more Fed buying/liquidity support suppresses yields), reinforcing the lower-yield case, though the analyst flags this could be a technical repo-driven spike that unwinds — moderate weight given acknowledged temporariness. Financial conditions tightened further (NFCI at -0.15, monotonic move), which is a yield-depressing flight-to-quality/tightening-of-credit signal, consistent with lower yields, though this driver is slower-moving and partly anticipated.

Taken together: inflation still falling, term premium's own level falling with negative momentum, balance sheet expansion (yield-suppressive), and tightening financial conditions (yield-suppressive) all point the same direction — down. Only labor tightness offers a mild anchor against that, and it's a "no change" signal rather than a hawkish one. This is a genuine shift from my prior near-flat carry; the term premium reversal (up→down) directly on the instrument I trade is the key trigger, corroborated by inflation and financial conditions. I am flipping my duration bet to modestly long (yields down = long duration, price up).

trade.rationale: Flip from near-flat to modest long duration: term premium's own level reversed lower, reinforced by continued disinflation, balance-sheet expansion, and tightening financial conditions all pointing to lower yields.

Given that a very favorable base-effect comparison is exhausted, and monthly momentum has stabilized rather than continued falling, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up at the next print rather than resuming its steep drop. This is a shift from my prior stance given the flattening in the yoy series and firming momentum data.

arbitrated.notes:
Since the last meeting several drivers flipped or softened, but the net rate-axis signal is still tilted lower, just with less conviction than before. The two drivers that actually move yields near-term — inflation and labor_tightness — are unchanged from last time: inflation is now flat (not falling further, base effects exhausted, core sticky) and labor stays flat/tight (unemployment at 12mo low, no loosening). Neither is giving a fresh disinflationary or labor-cooling impulse that would push yields down further, but neither is reaccelerating either — my stated flip trigger (CPI >0.5% MoM or core PCE re-accelerating >5.0%) was not hit. So the near-term reaction-function drivers argue for "flat," a mild fade from my prior "down" lean.

Slower, largely-priced drivers moved but in ways that only partially offset each other: balance_sheet flipped from "up" (bullish-duration read: growing balance sheet dampens term premium) to "down"/runoff (bearish for duration, more supply pressure), which is a genuine turn worth noting but it's a slow-moving, well-anticipated variable. Financial conditions eased off its tightening trend to flat, reducing the case for lower yields via risk-off channels. Term premium (10y specific) still leans down but with low, unconfirmed conviction and no fresh catalyst.

Net: the fast-moving drivers (inflation, labor) that dominate near-term repricing are neutral-to-flat, not confirming further declines. The slow drivers are mixed (balance sheet now bearish-duration, financial conditions neutral, term premium still mildly bullish-duration). This is not strong evidence to reverse my existing modest long-duration tilt, but it is enough to justify trimming conviction given the erosion of the disinflation/labor-cooling narrative that originally justified it. I'll hold the position but shrink size and conviction rather than flip.

arbitrated.notes: Relative to last meeting, the level case is more mixed, not more bearish-yields. Balance sheet runoff continues (down, conviction 0.65) — that's a supply-side force pushing yields up, and it's the freshest, most concrete driver this month. Term premium flipped from my prior "down" call to "flat/slightly up": 10y stalled at 3.64 after peaking at 3.83, 21d change is now positive, and the analyst explicitly flags a gap — it lacks visibility into balance-sheet runoff and financial conditions, both of which argue for firmer, not softer, long-end yields. That gap should be closed by leaning on balance_sheet's fresher, higher-conviction read rather than taking term_premium's own flat call at face value. Labor tightness is genuinely flat — 13 months rangebound, no drift, low trade-relevance despite decent conviction since it's already priced. Inflation (CPI/PCE) is flat-to-firmer near-term: base effects that drove the big y/y drop are fading, core PCE still elevated at 4.79%, m/m stabilized rather than collapsing — this is a live, near-term repricing driver and it argues mildly for higher, not lower, yields, a shift from my prior +0.05 lean. Financial conditions are easing (loosening), which is modestly yield-supportive on the duration-demand side but is a slow-moving, largely-priced driver — I discount its market impact. Net: the two things that actually move yields near-term — inflation persistence and balance-sheet supply — both point modestly higher, while term premium no longer confirms decline. My prior modest received-duration position was built on inflation expectations breaking down and balance sheet as the sole yields-up offset; with term premium now flat/up-leaning and inflation flat-not-falling, I should trim the duration long meaningfully rather than hold it, though panel disagreement (labor flat, conditions easing) argues against flipping outright short.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel is largely a continuation of last meeting's picture, with one meaningful shift: term_premium has firmed from "flat" to "up" (0.45), with 10yr yield near the top of its 12-month range and positive momentum across 21/63/252-day windows, plus a modestly positive 30s10s slope. This is the freshest, most rate-relevant read on offer since term_premium and its cousin curve_slope directly price the long end. Balance_sheet continues its firm, high-conviction "down" call (shrinking assets = less official-sector demand = upward yield pressure), consistent and undecelerating — this mechanically reinforces the term_premium call and closes the gap the term_premium analyst flagged (it lacks balance_sheet context, but balance_sheet's own read supports more, not less, supply pressure). Financial_conditions is "down" (easing) but conviction is low and momentum is decelerating/flattening — a slow, well-anticipated driver I discount for trade purposes. Inflation is "flat" with the base-effect tailwind fading, and labor_tightness is flat/tight-range-bound — both near-term repricing drivers are showing no strong signal, so I don't lean on them to shift level materially, though a stable-to-firm labor market and flat-to-up inflation lean slightly against lower yields.

Netting this out: the two live, fresh, high-signal drivers (term_premium up, balance_sheet down/shrinking) both push toward higher yields, while the slower financial_conditions easing is a weak offset that's largely priced. This is enough to nudge my prior mildly-short-duration stance slightly firmer, but not dramatically — no single new falsifier condition was fully triggered (yield pulled back from 3.85), so I resize modestly rather than reverse. I move from a token short-duration lean to a slightly larger, still-modest short-duration position, weighted toward the long end where term_premium and balance_sheet effects concentrate.

I do not yet see both 21d and 63d turning positive together, which was my stated reversal trigger. That confirms the up-move I flagged as a falsifier condition (a push above ~2.24-2.27) - it happened and then held rather than reverting. The 21d (+0.17) and 63d (+0.19) changes are solidly positive, though the 252d change is still slightly negative (-0.05), showing this is a recent shift rather than a sustained multi-quarter trend. Current level sits at roughly the midpoint of the 12-month range (0.52), 0.27pp above the 12m low and 0.37pp above the 2% target - not extreme, but no longer near the low end. The last five prints (2.38, 2.39, 2.36, 2.37, 2.38, 2.37) show the move has stalled and is oscillating in a narrow ~3bp band rather than continuing to climb. That argues against extrapolating further upside momentum for the next month; the level has likely found a new local equilibrium. I'll call this flat-to-slightly-up: the regime shift higher is real and priced in, but momentum has clearly faded.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The last print rose to 3.29% y/y from 3.07%, an uptick I flagged as a risk but called flat last time. That was wrong in direction terms - headline reaccelerated slightly. The mom print (0.20%) was between my thresholds, so it doesn't confirm strong reacceleration, but the yoy figure did move up meaningfully off the 12m low. Base effects are the key driver: headline yoy fell hard over the prior year (8.22->3.07) mostly from energy/base effects rolling off, and now that tailwind is fading - headline_mom_3m_avg (0.19%) annualizes to ~2.35%, still near target, but the most recent yoy uptick plus core running hotter (core_3m_annualized 3.68% vs core yoy 4.38%, still 2.38pp above target) suggests further base-effect exhaustion is likely to push yoy higher in the next print too, absent a soft mom reading. Core continues to decelerate only slowly (-0.40pp over 3m) and remains the better read on persistence - it's still elevated and sticky, arguing against a benign path for headline. Given the recent yoy uptick, the fading of favorable base effects, and core stickiness, I lean toward another slight increase in headline yoy at the next release, though momentum data alone (mom 0.20%, 3m avg 0.19%) doesn't support a sharp move.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% would confirm base effects aren't reaccelerating; above 0.35% would confirm a stronger uptrend.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline

The 3-month change of +0.10pp is trivial noise within a 0.20pp range and the Sahm-gap of 0.17pp is far below the 0.50pp threshold that would signal genuine loosening. The series has oscillated between 3.40 and 3.60 with no directional persistence — essentially a flat, tight labor market holding roughly 0.50pp below the natural rate. Policy language reinforces this: "job gains have been robust... unemployment rate has remained low," with only a forward-looking mention of credit tightening as a risk, not evidence of current loosening. Absent a break outside the established range, the base case for the next report is continuation of this tight, rangebound pattern rather than a directional move. I maintain a flat call, keeping conviction moderate given the small but real risk that credit tightening eventually shows up in hiring data.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% (new 12-month high) would confirm loosening; a drop to 3.40% or below would suggest renewed tightening beyond noise.

My previous "up" call was correct — yields continued to climb through this window. Given continued near-cycle-high levels, intact upward momentum, and no reversal signal in the slope or level, I maintain a modestly bullish (rising) call for the long-end term premium into next month-end, though the deceleration in 21d momentum warrants tempering conviction slightly.

Balance sheet: runoff continues monotonically at steady pace with no policy language change — a slow, well-anticipated driver, but its direction (shrinking) still supports higher long-end term premium via supply, even though its own headline (assets) is falling clearly. Financial conditions continue to ease (NFCI falling), which is disinflationary for policy pressure but also a background, slow-moving signal already largely priced; modest weight. arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting the near-term repricing drivers have diverged from my prior read while the slow, priced-in drivers keep grinding in the same direction. Inflation: headline CPI reaccelerated (yoy 3.29→3.72%, m/m 0.48%), confirming and strengthening the prior "up" call — this is the freshest, most market-moving data point and argues for higher, not lower, yields. Labor: unemployment jumped to a 12-month high (3.70%), the largest single-month move in the series — a genuine loosening signal, which is disinflationary/dovish and pulls the other way, tempering the inflation push. This is a live driver that directly repositions expectations for Fed policy and thus yields. Term premium's own analyst now says the "up" call has stalled — 10y momentum flattened near-term (21d ~flat) even though the 63d/252d trend remains up; I read this as a fading tailwind rather than a reversal, so close to neutral-to-mild-up. Financial conditions continue to ease (NFCI at 52w low, accelerating), which mechanically supports slightly higher yields at the margin (looser conditions = less need for safety bid), but this is a slow-moving, largely-priced driver. Balance sheet: QT continues unabated, a structural supply-side upward pressure on term premium, but also slow-moving and already anticipated. Netting the fresh, repricing-relevant drivers (inflation up, labor loosening down, term premium fading-but-not-reversed) against the slower ones (financial conditions easing up, balance sheet down QT up), I see a modest tilt toward the "up" side surviving, but with more offset than last meeting given the labor loosening. This argues for trimming, not flipping, my prior modest short-duration (higher-yield) position.

arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting the picture has shifted meaningfully on the two drivers that actually move yields near-term, while the slow structural drivers stayed on their prior trajectories. Inflation: headline CPI yoy stalled (3.72→3.69) rather than reaccelerating as I'd called, but the 3m-annualized pace (4.36%) still runs hotter than the yoy print and core PCE keeps decelerating — a genuinely mixed, low-conviction picture, best read as flat-to-mild-up, not the confident up I carried. Labor: unemployment plateaued at 3.70% (12-month high) rather than continuing to loosen or reversing — momentum has stalled, so this is closer to flat than my prior +0.45 "up" (tightening) call implied. Both of the live, repricing-relevant drivers have thus faded toward neutral versus last meeting. Term premium is the one driver that has genuinely turned against my carried short-duration tilt: 10y at 12-month highs, sustained +47bp/21d and +78bp/63d momentum, curve confirming a genuine long-end move rather than pure curve-shape. This is a real, fresh signal for higher yields. Balance sheet continues its steady, well-anticipated runoff (down assets → structurally higher term premium/duration supply), consistent with and reinforcing the term-premium call, but this is slow-moving and largely priced. Financial conditions have stabilized near cycle lows — no longer easing, but not tightening either — a wash for the level view. Net: the two fast, near-term drivers (inflation, labor) have washed out toward neutral, removing the modest disinflationary tailwind I'd been leaning on, while term premium has turned more clearly toward higher yields with fresh, sustained momentum. This argues for reducing the short-duration position toward flat rather than maintaining or deepening it — the evidence for lower yields has weakened, not strengthened.

Looking at the full picture now: headline yoy has round-tripped from a mid-series bump (3.29→3.72→3.69) back down to 3.25, essentially near the 12-month low of 3.07. Core PCE continues a steady, uninterrupted descent (3.83→3.69), now 1.69pp above target, with 3m annualized core running at just 2.26% — close to target-consistent pace. Headline 3m annualized (4.12%) is noisier and still elevated, but the latest single-month print was soft (0.14% m/m, below the 3m average of 0.34%). The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is small and positive, but the outright yoy drop of 0.44pp in one month is the dominant signal. Weighting core for persistence (given headline's energy/food noise) and noting the actual outcome versus my prior expectation, the momentum is disinflationary, not stalling. I don't see strong evidence for a rebound next month — no base effects or acceleration signals here — so I expect headline yoy to be flat-to-slightly-down, not up.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or yoy rising back above 3.6% would signal renewed acceleration rather than continued disinflation.

  The prior call was flat with modest conviction, and it was wrong: the newest print jumped to 3.90% from 3.70%, exactly the kind of push above 3.80% I flagged as the trigger for renewed loosening. The unemployment rate has now risen 0.40pp over 3 months and 0.50pp over 6 months, sitting at the 12-month high and a full 0.50pp above the 12-month low (3.40%). The Sahm-style gap (0.37pp) is approaching levels historically associated with turning points. This is not a single noisy tick — the last three readings (3.70, 3.70, 3.90) show consecutive increases after a period of flat oscillation between 3.40-3.60%, suggesting a genuine trend shift rather than measurement noise. While the level itself (3.90%) is still only slightly above a 4% natural rate estimate, the rate-of-change is the more important signal here per the Sahm-rule spirit, and it argues for continued loosening rather than stabilization. Policy language also shifted from "robust" to "slowed" job gains, consistent with cooling. I'm revising toward a directional call: the unemployment rate is more likely to tick up or hold at this elevated level than to reverse sharply back down next month, given the momentum evident in the trailing three prints.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 3.60-3.70% would confirm the 3.90 print was noise; another rise to 4.0%+ would confirm accelerating loosening.

arbitrated.notes: The panel largely confirms and modestly extends the up-in-yields read I carried last meeting, but with a notable shift in composition: labor is now the swing factor pulling the other way. Term premium remains up (10y near cycle highs, 4.88, strong 63d/252d momentum) though its own analyst flags the most recent prints are flattening — the sharpest leg may be pausing. Balance sheet continues its steady, unambiguous runoff (down, -0.80 conviction), which structurally supports higher term premium via reduced duration absorption — this is the named gap the term_premium analyst itself flagged (it lacks balance_sheet), and the runoff data closes that gap in the direction term_premium already assumed: supportive of its up call. Financial conditions have genuinely turned — a six-print consecutive tightening streak reversing the prior easing trend, which is a live, non-stale signal and modestly supports higher yields (tighter conditions historically coincide with higher long rates, though it's a lagging/coincident confirmation rather than a lead). The key change from last meeting: inflation has confirmed disinflation (yoy 3.69→3.25, core PCE at 2.26% 3m annualized, near target pace) — my prior 'up' lean was already conservative in the last cutoff; this now clearly argues down, not just flat. And labor has flipped hard: unemployment jumped 3.70→3.90, a Sahm-adjacent trend break, arguing for a *looser* labor market, which is disinflationary and yield-negative via the Fed reaction function, despite the analyst's headline sign convention being "up" (unemployment rising = tightening... nc, labor_tightness analyst's "up" call means unemployment rising, i.e., market loosening, which pulls yields down). This is a fresher, more repricing-relevant read than the slow-moving balance sheet or financial conditions series. Netting these: term premium and balance sheet still argue for higher yields structurally, but inflation cooling and labor loosening — the two near-term reaction-function drivers — now argue for lower yields with real conviction and freshness. This is enough to trim, not reverse, my long-yield tilt.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has shifted meaningfully since last meeting, and mostly in a direction that reinforces my existing modest long-duration lean rather than reversing it. Term premium flipped from "up" to "flat" — the analyst explicitly says the 10y has dropped to 4.37 with a sharp negative 21d change, directly hitting the falsifier I set last time ("10y break below 4.45 confirms the down case"). That's a real, fresh, and rate-relevant signal: it confirms rather than contradicts my prior read. Financial conditions flipped from "up" (tightening) to "down" (easing) — also a reversal that removes the one driver that was pulling against my long-duration lean. Inflation continues its disinflationary grind (headline yoy 3.13, mom 0.15%) with a caveat about diminishing base effects, but the CPI m/m falsifier threshold (0.35%) was not breached — no flip trigger here. Labor tightness pulled back from 3.90% to 3.70%, squarely in the "noise" band I flagged as not confirming a flip toward higher yields; the analyst downgraded conviction and calls it borderline. Balance sheet remains a strong, clear, but slow-moving structural upward pressure on yields — priced in and non-catalytic near-term, so I keep it as context, not a lever. Curve slope is not my seat.

Netting this out: two of the more rate-relevant, live drivers (term premium, financial conditions) both moved toward "lower yields" and away from where they stood last meeting, while labor and inflation stayed within their no-flip bands. Nothing in this panel argues for reversing my modest long-duration position; if anything the case for holding it — or leaning slightly more into it — has strengthened. I'll hold size roughly flat, nudging conviction up slightly given the term premium/financial conditions confirmation, without over-committing given labor/inflation are still just "flat" reads, not confirmations of a big move down.

arbitrated.notes: The panel largely confirms rather than overturns last meeting's read, though several drivers refreshed with nuance. Balance sheet remains the clearest, freshest, highest-conviction signal: QT continues on an unbroken monotonic path with no sign of taper — a structural, persistent upward force on term premium/long-end yields that is NOT yet fully priced given its steady grinding nature. Financial conditions continue to ease (looser, fresh 52w low) which is a modest offsetting downward pull on yields via risk appetite, but this is a slow-moving, largely-anticipated driver and I discount its market impact accordingly. Inflation: headline CPI yoy surprised up but core PCE continues to decelerate — net picture is flat-to-mild-disinflation, not a repricing catalyst; low trade-relevance weight given deceleration in core continues. Labor tightness is the one driver that has genuinely moved against my prior "tightening" (+0.10) call: unemployment has drifted to 3.80%, near Sahm-trigger territory, a sustained multi-month loosening trend, not noise. This is the most trade-relevant near-term driver (labor feeds the reaction function directly) and it now points toward looser labor markets, which is dovish for yields — I flip my labor conviction to negative (loosening, own-measurement direction) which argues for lower, not higher, front-end yields. Term premium itself is muddled: multi-week decline dominates but a small recent bounce is inconclusive, and it lacks balance-sheet and financial-conditions context per the missing-inputs flag — I lean on balance_sheet's fresher, more decisive read to fill that gap, tilting term premium mildly upward structurally but not enough to overturn a still-negative net level view. Net: balance sheet's structural upward pull is real but slow and partially priced; labor loosening and easy financial conditions argue for yields drifting modestly lower still. This is consistent with my prior read — no driver has flipped decisively enough to reverse the small long position, but the labor shift + easier financial conditions modestly reinforce the downward lean, so I nudge conviction up slightly while keeping the position small given persistent disagreement between balance-sheet (up-pressure) and labor/financial-conditions (down-pressure).

discounted.why: Headline/core conflict; net flat, no clear repricing catalyst this cycle.
Conflicting signals, missing gap inputs; deferred to balance_sheet and financial_conditions for structural read.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI m/m print above 0.35%, unemployment reversing back below 3.60%, or QT pause/taper language would flip this toward higher yields.

risks.text: QT pace or Fed language could shift abruptly at next meeting, reversing the structural upward pull.
Labor loosening could stall or reverse if next payrolls print surprises tighter.
Panel disagreement between balance-sheet (up) and labor/FC (down) means level signal is genuinely weak.
Easing financial conditions could tighten sharply if risk-off flows reverse, undercutting the downward lean.

trade.rationale: Hold prior small long-duration tilt: labor loosening and easing financial conditions modestly offset structural QT-driven upward pressure; panel split keeps size small, unchanged from last meeting.

why.balance_sheet: Unbroken monotonic runoff, no pause language; own measurement (assets) falls, structural upward pressure on yields persists.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI at fresh 52w low, steady easing; own measurement falls further, slow/priced driver, modest downward pull on yields.
why.inflation: Headline yoy ticked up but core PCE still decelerating; net wash, low conviction on own-measurement direction.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment drifted to 3.80%, near Sahm threshold, sustained loosening trend; flips prior tightening call, most trade-relevant driver.
why.term_premium: Small recent bounce off multi-week decline; muddled, lacks balance-sheet/FC context, lean mildly up but low conviction.

  The position you are carrying: DGS10 +0.03, DGS2 +0.02 (conviction 0.25).
  You said this read would flip if: A CPI m/m print above 0.35%, unemployment reversing back below 3.60%, or QT pause/taper language would flip this toward higher yields.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last 13 weekly prints show continued balance sheet runoff, though the pace has decelerated markedly. Total assets fell from ~7.867T to 7.677T over the window, with 13w change -230.6B and 52w change -793.3B confirming a sustained downtrend. However, the most recent 4-week change is only -35.6B (about -8.9B/week average), noticeably slower than the 13w average pace of roughly -17.7B/week, and the last two prints (7,673,741 then 7,677,230) show a small uptick week-over-week. This could reflect normal noise around a slowing runoff cap, month-end settlement effects, or early signs of reserve-scarcity-driven deceleration, but it is not yet a reversal. Policy language remains unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued balance-sheet reduction per previously announced plans — no taper or pause has been announced. My prior "down" call was directionally correct: assets did continue falling. Given the unchanged policy language and still-negative 4w/13w/52w trends, I maintain a down call for month-end, but with reduced conviction given the recent deceleration and the tiny uptick in the last observation, which warrants watching for a possible pace change or pause signal.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive weekly rises above recent levels, or policy statement announcing pause/taper/end of runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (steepening/up, conviction 0.50) was largely wrong for the most recent leg: after peaking near -0.14, the spread fell back to -0.30 then -0.28, a renewed flattening over the last two prints. The 21d change is still marginally positive (+0.07) but the 63d change is now negative (-0.13), showing the steepening impulse has faded and partially reversed. The 252d change (+0.42) confirms the multi-quarter steepening trend from deep inversion is intact structurally, and slope_range_position (0.84) shows we're still near the high end of the 12-month range (least-inverted levels), well above the 12m low. However, the most recent two observations are the least steep in the last several prints, indicating short-term momentum has turned down. Policy language shifted from emphasizing "slowed growth" and "additional policy firming" to "solid pace" expansion and no imminent cuts until greater confidence on inflation — this leans toward the Fed holding higher for longer, which typically supports front-end anchoring but doesn't clearly dictate long-end direction. Given the conflicting signals — longer-term steepening trend vs. recent 2-print pullback and negative 63d momentum — I lean toward a modest flattening bias near-term, but without high conviction given the level is still far from extreme inversion and range position is high.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.16 with slope_change_21d and 63d both turning positive would confirm resumed steepening and reverse this call.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady, uninterrupted grind lower, now at -0.39, a fresh 52-week low, with nfci_range_position at 0.00. My prior call (down) was correct: the index has moved from -0.28 to -0.39 over this window, and the last observation (-0.39) is the lowest of the entire 13-print run, showing no stalling or reversal — the falsifier condition (a sustained rise back toward -0.32+) did not occur. Momentum remains negative: 4-week change is -0.03 and 13-week change is -0.10, both consistently negative with no deceleration visible in the last few prints (-0.36, -0.36, -0.37, -0.38, -0.39). Conditions are now well below the zero average, indicating looser-than-average financial conditions, and the trend shows no sign of bottoming. The policy language describes past tightening effects still working through the economy but does not itself signal a shift in current financial conditions data. Given the persistence and lack of any inflection, I expect this easing trend to continue into month end, though I temper conviction slightly since the pace of decline is modest and could flatten given how far the index already sits from its average.
Would change its mind if: A rise back toward -0.34 or higher sustained over 2+ weeks would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two prints are flat at 2.26, following a small step down from 2.30. My prior "down" call at 0.35 conviction was directionally consistent with the 63-day and 252-day trends (-0.18pp, -0.03pp), but the most recent data shows stabilization rather than continued decline: the series has been essentially pinned at 2.26-2.30 for the last several observations, and the 21-day change is actually +0.10pp, reflecting the earlier bounce before settling. The breakeven remains 0.26pp above the 2% target, sitting at 0.38 of its 12-month range (2.10-2.52), which is roughly the middle-to-lower-middle of the band — not signaling strong momentum in either direction. There's no evidence of a break below the 12-month low or a sustained push higher; the two most recent readings being identical suggests the series has found a local equilibrium after its modest pullback from 2.34. Given the flattening in the last two prints and the absence of fresh momentum signals in either direction, I'm revising toward flat. The prior down call is losing support as the decline appears to have stalled rather than accelerated.
Would change its mind if: A move below 2.20 sustained over multiple prints would revive the downside case; a push above 2.34 would signal renewed upside momentum.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked up to 3.09% from 3.32%... wait, actually the newest value (3.09) is below the prior (3.32), so headline actually fell, not rose — my prior "flat" call undershot the disinflation that continued. The latest m/m print (0.31%) was below my 0.35% acceleration threshold, so no trigger was hit, consistent with holding flat-to-down. Headline y/y sits at 3.09%, near its 12-month low (3.07%), and core PCE y/y continues a steady descent to 3.11% from 3.29%, a -0.58pp move over three months. Core 3m annualized (1.79%) is now below target-consistent pace, and headline 3m annualized (2.63%) is also much cooler than the trailing y/y suggests. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is negative (-0.13pp), meaning the latest month's pace decelerated versus the recent average. Policy language was revised to acknowledge "inflation has eased over the past year," a explicit acknowledgment of the disinflation trend, though "remains elevated" is retained. Combining soft 3-month annualized rates, continued core y/y decline, and the low position in the 12-month range, the momentum is disinflationary. Headline could still tick up modestly on base effects (given the sequence of past m/m prints rolling off), but the underlying trend and 3m pace point to flat-to-down for the headline next print.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.40% or core PCE y/y ticking back up above 3.3% would confirm reacceleration rather than continued disinflation.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.45) was directionally wrong for the latest print: unemployment fell from 3.90% to 3.70%, right in the mean-reversion range I flagged as the falsifier. The series is noisy around 3.5-3.9% with no clean trend: 3m change is now -0.20pp (tightening), while 6m and 12m changes are still +0.20pp (mild loosening from a year ago). The Sahm gap of 0.33pp is well below the 0.50 trigger threshold, and the level itself (3.70%) is only modestly above the 4% natural-rate anchor (-0.30pp distance), so this is not a tight labor market by historical standards but also not showing accelerating deterioration. Range position (0.60) shows we're in the upper-middle of the 12-month band, consistent with oscillation rather than a breakout. Given the whipsaw pattern (3.9→3.7→3.8→3.7), the most defensible read is that the market is bouncing within a narrow band rather than trending decisively either way. I'll shade slightly toward flat/up given the still-positive 6m/12m drift, but with much lower conviction than before since the last move confirmed noise, not signal.
Would change its mind if: A print below 3.60% would confirm renewed tightening; a print above 3.90% would confirm a genuine breakout and accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y sits at 3.99%, having declined further from the prior release. Looking at the recent path: 4.14→4.15→4.11→4.14→4.18→4.14→4.15→4.08→4.06→3.99 — a clear downtrend in the last four readings, with the level breaking below 4.0%. The 63-day change is -0.89pp, a substantial decline, though the 252-day change is still +0.44pp, so the longer-term trend remains upward even as the recent move is down. Range position is 0.41, roughly mid-range, not extreme. The 21d change is +0.11pp, but that's stale relative to the very recent slide to 3.99 — the last three prints (4.08, 4.06, 3.99) show accelerating downward momentum. Slope 30s10s at +0.23pp is modestly positive but below my prior +0.25pp steepening threshold, so no confirmation of an up-move via curve steepening. My prior 'flat' call was wrong — the yield broke decisively below 4.0% with clear negative recent momentum, which is closer to my own down-confirmation criterion (albeit not quite through 3.79%). Given the fresh break below 4.0% and negative momentum in the last three observations, I lean toward continued softness in the term premium proxy through month end, though I stop short of high conviction since the longer trend and range position don't scream extreme.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.10% with 21d momentum turning positive and 30s10s steepening further would reverse this down call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_21d, slope_30s10s, dgs10_range_position

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has moved meaningfully since last meeting, and mostly in the direction that reinforces my carried view, though for a different mix of reasons. Term premium (10y itself) has broken decisively below 4.0% with accelerating downward momentum over the last three prints — this is the single freshest, most trade-relevant read and it points down on yields. Financial conditions (NFCI) continues its uninterrupted grind to fresh 52-week lows, a live, non-stale signal of continued easing in financial conditions, which itself is a lagging confirmation of lower yields/looser conditions rather than a forward driver, but its persistence with no inflection is notable. Inflation is confirmed cooling — core PCE y/y down to 3.11%, 3m annualized pace below target-consistent, disinflationary momentum intact — this is the most trade-relevant near-term driver alongside labor, and it argues for yields drifting lower, not higher. Labor tightness, however, is genuinely noisy: unemployment whipsawing 3.9→3.7→3.8→3.7 with conviction down to 0.20 — I treat this as near-abstention, no real signal either way. Balance sheet still shows runoff but decelerating sharply, with a tentative uptick in the last two prints; policy language unchanged, so I keep a mild down (i.e., mildly yield-supportive-to-neutral from QT) but discount its weight given deceleration and its inherent slowness/anticipation.

Net: the fresher, faster-moving drivers (term premium's own level break, inflation's continued disinflation) both point toward lower yields, while labor is too noisy to use and balance sheet/financial conditions are slower-moving confirmations rather than new information. This tips me from my prior near-neutral/slightly-up carry toward a modest downward duration bias — a change justified by the term-premium's fresh technical break and inflation's continued cooling, not merely by a single month's price move.

Fed language ('solid pace,' no imminent cuts) could keep front-end anchored even as long end drifts lower.
Crowded long-duration positioning after a fast rally raises risk of a sharp snapback.
Balance sheet deceleration or a pause signal could reverse the financial-conditions easing tailwind.

trade.rationale: Fresh term-premium break below 4.0% plus continued disinflation and easing financial conditions argue for a modest downward duration bias; shift from prior flat/slightly-up carry given this meeting's live evidence.

  The previous "down" call at 0.35 conviction was correct in direction: the spread moved from roughly -0.34/-0.36 into -0.41 before a modest bounce to -0.37/-0.39 in the last two prints. Net over the last two readings the curve has flattened slightly further versus the prior release, though the most recent two ticks (-0.37, -0.39) show a small give-back of the deepest inversion rather than continued flattening. Given the persistence and monotonicity of the decline, the most probable outcome at month-end is a continuation of this easing trend, i.e., a further modest decline in NFCI. There is no evidence in the data of deceleration — the pace of weekly decline has if anything been stable to slightly accelerating in recent prints (-0.01 per week average, consistent throughout). arbitrated.notes:
The panel has moved meaningfully against my prior duration-lower call, and I need to update. Term premium flipped from "down" to "up" (0.35) with the 10y explicitly breaking above my own falsifier level (4.10%) into a 4.24-4.33 band with positive momentum — that is a direct hit on the trigger I set last meeting. Inflation also flipped from down to up (0.45) on hotter headline CPI m/m prints and a small core PCE uptick, again crossing into territory I flagged as reversing my disinflation thesis. Labor tightness continues to show gradual loosening (unemployment rising to 3.90%, up conviction 0.45) — but a rising unemployment rate is disinflationary/dovish for yields, cutting against the inflation and term-premium signals, so it partially offsets them rather than reinforcing my prior short-yields view. Balance sheet (down/QT continuing) and financial conditions (down/easing) are both slow-moving, well-anticipated series with high conviction but low near-term repricing power — QT is priced in gradually and NFCI easing, while directionally rate-negative in isolation, is offset by the fact it also signals looser conditions that don't obviously pull yields down (easier conditions can coexist with higher yields).

The two live, fast-moving drivers that actually reprice yields — inflation and term premium — both turned higher this meeting, and both explicitly cite crossing my own stated flip triggers. That is a named, meeting-specific reason to reduce, not maintain, my short-duration stance. Labor loosening is a genuine offset but it's a slower, more gradual signal (12m trend) versus the sharper 3-month inflation acceleration. Net: I flip from net-lower-yields to a much smaller, near-flat-to-mildly-higher-yields lean, sized down given the panel disagreement (labor pulls one way, inflation/term premium the other).

arbitrated.notes:
Relative to last meeting, the panel has softened almost across the board. Inflation is the one live, trade-relevant driver still pushing yields up: headline CPI reaccelerated (three straight strong m/m prints, 3m annualized 4.75%) with high conviction, though the analyst itself flags this as base-effect/energy-driven while core PCE keeps decelerating — a more mixed underlying signal than the headline number alone suggests. Labor tightness plateaued at 3.90% rather than continuing to rise; conviction dropped to 0.30 and the call is now "modest softening bias" rather than a clear tightening or loosening signal, so it's a weak, near-neutral input this month. Term premium flipped from up to flat, with 21d momentum now negative and level drifting down to 4.20 — a fresher read than last month's "up" call, and it directly contradicts the duration-up thesis I was carrying. Financial conditions similarly stalled (flat, deceleration in easing) rather than continuing to ease, which is a mild net-neutral-to-slightly-supportive-of-higher-yields signal but weak. Balance sheet continues its steady, well-anticipated runoff — high conviction but slow-moving and already priced, so I discount its market impact even though the reading itself is unambiguous.

Netting these: the only driver with both a live read and real near-term repricing power (inflation) still leans toward higher yields, but its own momentum and the offsetting term-premium/labor plateau readings are weaker than last month. arbitrated.notes:
This panel largely confirms the prior read rather than reversing it. Inflation (formed 15 days ago) shows headline m/m cooling to 0.22% and y/y ticking down to 3.36%, with core PCE stalling near 3.1% — this is a genuine softening from my prior +0.55 conviction and is the most trade-relevant near-term repricing driver since it's the freshest, highest-impact input. Labor tightness stayed flat at 3.90%, a plateau at cycle highs — not loosening further, but not the acceleration my prior +0.15 implied either; I'm marking this down toward near-zero given the stall. Term premium is the loudest call (up, 0.45) built on real yield momentum (10y drifting 4.59→4.69, accelerating on 21d/63d/252d windows), but the panel itself flags this call is missing balance_sheet and financial_conditions context — both of which came in this meeting as continued-QT-as-expected and rangebound/flat respectively, i.e. nothing incrementally new to justify fresh upward pressure beyond what's already priced. Balance sheet continues its steady, fully-anticipated runoff (~$82B/4wk) — a slow, well-telegraphed driver I discount for trading purposes per the framework, despite high conviction. Financial conditions remain rangebound/flat, tiny drift, not trade-relevant.

Netting this: the freshest, most decision-relevant signal (inflation) has cooled versus last meeting, arguing against the higher-yields tilt I held; term premium's momentum is real but is already reflected in current levels and lacks confirmation from the two structural drivers it depends on. Labor is flat-to-plateaued, a wash. I'm trimming my long-duration-short (higher yield) position modestly — not reversing — since no single driver has decisively flipped, but the balance of freshly-reported evidence leans slightly against further yield increases.

But the hard data — especially the low outgoing m/m and continued core PCE decline — argues for at least a modest downward drift in next month's headline y/y, absent a rebound. I lean modestly toward a lower headline print next month, tempered by base-effect and monthly noise risk.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or core PCE y/y rebounding above 3.2% would reverse this call toward flat/up.

My prior flat call was correct: the series did not breach 4.00% nor drop back to 3.70-3.80%, it simply stayed put at the recent high. That said, the broader trend context still matters: unrate_12m_low was 3.50%, and we're now 0.40pp above that low (sahm_gap +0.40pp), sitting at the top of the 12-month range (range_position 1.00). The 6m and 12m changes (+0.20pp, +0.30pp) show a gradual loosening drift over the past year, even though the most recent 3 months show zero net movement. The level itself (3.90%) is essentially at the 4% natural-rate benchmark, not stretched either way. With three consecutive flat prints at the cycle high, the signal is one of stabilization at a slightly elevated level rather than acceleration in either direction. Absent a fresh move, the most defensible call for the next report is flat, though the plateau at the range ceiling means any tick up would confirm renewed loosening while any tick down would suggest genuine improvement.
Would change its mind if: A print at 4.00%+ would confirm renewed loosening; a drop back to 3.70-3.80% would confirm genuine reversal rather than plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_3m_avg, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

The level (4.51%) remains elevated versus the 12-month low (3.61%) and closer to the high (4.98%), so the medium-term uptrend in term premium is intact, but short-term momentum has clearly cooled. The Fed's announced slowing of Treasury runoff (cap cut from $60bn to $25bn) reduces net supply pressure from balance-sheet reduction, which should modestly ease upward pressure on long-end yields going forward. Given the conflicting signals — medium-term uptrend vs. short-term reversal plus reduced runoff pressure — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-lower call for the next month, with low-to-moderate conviction given the mixed picture.
Would change its mind if: A renewed push above 4.60% with positive 21d momentum and further 30s10s steepening would revive the up case.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has shifted meaningfully from last meeting, and mostly toward a lower-yield synthesis than my prior modest-up position implied. Term premium — my prior strongest driver at +0.35 — now reports flat-to-slightly-lower: the 10y has pulled back from 4.61 to 4.51 with negative 21d momentum, and the analyst explicitly flags reduced Treasury supply from the QT taper as a dampener on long-end pressure going forward. That's a live, fresh read directly contradicting the prior up-call, and it closes the very gap I'd flagged (term_premium needing balance_sheet context) — the analyst itself now incorporates the taper. Balance sheet remains down (contraction continues) but conviction is now explicitly tempered because the Committee has slowed the runoff pace — a QT taper is a smaller upward push on term premium than before, consistent with the term_premium analyst's own read. Inflation continues to lean down: low m/m prints, core PCE grinding toward target, though policy rhetoric shows a mild hawkish tilt worth flagging as a risk. Labor tightness is flat/plateaued at 3.90% — a stabilization signal, not a strong directional push either way. Financial conditions remain flat and stable, a slow-moving, well-priced driver I discount for trade purposes.

Netting this out: the two live, near-term repricing drivers (inflation trending down, term premium losing its upward momentum with a supply-side tailwind from tapered QT) both point toward easing pressure on yields, while balance-sheet contraction continuing provides a smaller, decelerating counterweight. This is a genuine shift versus last meeting's setup, where term premium was the dominant up-driver. I am reducing my long-duration tilt toward roughly flat, with a very slight lean toward lower yields, given the momentum reversal in the 10y and the confirmed inflation cooling — but conviction stays low given the panel's internal split (labor flat, financial conditions flat, balance sheet still down).

None of my reversal triggers (m/m above 0.35% or core PCE y/y above 3.2%) were hit — in fact both moved further in the disinflationary direction. Given a negative m/m print and broad-based deceleration in both core and headline gauges, I expect headline CPI y/y to be flat-to-slightly-lower next month, though the very low base for m/m comparisons a year ago could produce some year-over-year noise in either direction, tempering confidence in a strong further decline.

This was not a one-off wobble: the 3m change (+0.20pp), 6m change (+0.30pp), and 12m change (+0.50pp) all point the same direction, and the rate is now sitting at the 12-month high, at the top of its range (range_position 1.00). The Sahm-rule-style gap (current vs 3m avg trough) is 0.47pp, uncomfortably close to the classic 0.5pp recession-signal threshold, indicating this is a rapid rise off the trailing low rather than noise. Distance from the ~4% natural rate is now +0.10pp, meaning we've crossed from tight into modestly loose territory. Policy language still describes job gains as "strong" and unemployment as "low," but the subtle rewording — risks "have moved toward better balance over the past year" (past tense) versus previously "are moving" (present, ongoing) — reads as an acknowledgment that the labor side has already shifted, consistent with the data. I was wrong to call flat last time; the plateau broke to the upside. Given acceleration and level both now unfavorable, I lean toward further softening next month, though one print is not yet a firm trend.
Would change its mind if: A pullback to 3.90% or below would suggest the 4.10% print was noise rather than a genuine trend break.

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting, several drivers that were pushing yields lower have either turned or faded, while the labor and term-premium reads — the ones that actually move the rate axis — have shifted toward higher yields. Labor tightness flipped decisively: unemployment jumped to a 12-month high (4.10%, Sahm gap 0.47pp), which is a loosening labor market and historically the most reliable near-term trigger for lower policy-rate expectations — but the panel's own conviction here (0.55, "up" in unemployment) signals rising slack, which argues for lower yields on the front end via Fed-easing expectations. That's a genuine, fresh, high-impact signal I need to respect even though it cuts against my prior long-end call. Inflation continues to soften (CPI y/y 2.97%, core PCE 2.77%, negative m/m) — also disinflationary for yields, reinforcing the case for lower/flat yields on the front end. However, term premium ticked up (10y at 4.36, mild uptrend) and financial conditions drifted incrementally tighter — both modest, slow-moving, largely already-priced signals. Balance sheet is still shrinking but decelerating (cap cut from $60B to $25B), a modest reduction in QT-driven long-end supply pressure, which is disinflationary/dovish for term premium, not something that should push yields higher.

Netting this out: the two live, repricing-relevant drivers (labor loosening, inflation cooling) both point toward lower yields, while the slower drivers (term premium, financial conditions) lean mildly higher but are lower-impact and partially anticipated. This tips the balance further toward lower yields than last meeting, not less — the disinflation and labor-loosening signals have both strengthened rather than reversed. I am deepening my modest short-yield stance slightly, still capped by real disagreement in the panel (curve_slope steepening trend, term premium drift up) and no acceleration/breakout confirmed anywhere.

arbitrated.notes: Since last meeting, the two live, near-term repricing drivers have both flipped or strengthened in the direction of lower yields. Inflation continues to cool (headline CPI y/y at a 12-month low, core PCE easing, no re-acceleration in the details) — reaffirming last month's down call. Labor tightness has now clearly turned: unemployment rose again to 4.20%, a full 0.50pp off its 12-month low, with accelerating 3m/6m momentum — this reverses my prior read that labor was tightening (I had it +0.55 last time, meaning I was long the "tightening" side, which was wrong-footed). A rising unemployment rate means loosening labor, which is disinflationary/yield-negative, not yield-positive. That is the single biggest change in my read this month and it reinforces rather than contradicts the inflation and term-premium signals. Term premium itself has now flipped to down (10y fell from 4.28 to 4.09, breaking its own upside trigger), confirming that long-end richening is real and not just my own driver's noise — this is a fresh, live cross-confirmation. Financial conditions continue a slow, modest tightening grind (mostly priced, low market-moving value) and balance-sheet runoff continues on its steady, well-anticipated path — both are background/slow-moving and I discount their standalone rate impact. Given labor loosening (via rising unemployment), disinflation, and confirmed term-premium decline all point the same way — lower yields — while financial conditions and balance sheet pull only mildly against via slow-moving channels, I lean toward extending the modest short-duration-yield (long price) stance I already carry, sized up slightly given the labor reversal is now unambiguous and cross-confirmed by term premium.

Given the level is now meaningfully above the recent low and off natural-rate anchor, and there's no sign of reversal back toward 4.00% or below, I lean toward continued softness but with reduced conviction versus a clean upward call, since the immediate momentum has stalled.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 4.00% or below, or a third consecutive flat print at 4.20%, would confirm stabilization rather than continued loosening.

Given the recent short-term reversal higher, proximity to 12-month lows, and continued QT language, I lean toward a modest further rise into month end, though the medium-term downtrend keeps conviction moderate rather than high.

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting, the two drivers that actually move nominal yields near-term — labor and inflation — have both softened their prior downward-yield calls without reversing them: unemployment is flat at 4.20% for two straight prints (loosening trend intact but momentum stalled, conviction only 0.35 vs 0.55 prior), and headline CPI's sharp drop looks like it was partly base effects, with core PCE actually ticking up and inflation now called "flat" rather than "down." That's a genuine softening of the disinflationary/labor-loosening case that anchored my prior -0.12/-0.12 duration-long position. Meanwhile term_premium flipped from "down" to "up" (conviction 0.45) on a real short-term reversal in the 10y (3.79→3.91), though it's explicitly missing balance_sheet and financial_conditions context and its medium-term trend is still down — so I treat this as a live but partial signal, not decisive. Balance sheet continues clean, high-conviction runoff (down, 0.62) — trade-relevant for term premium/supply pressure at the long end but slow-moving and largely anticipated, so I discount its standalone yield impact. Financial conditions remains a weak, wobbly signal (tightening tick, 0.35 conviction) within a multi-month range — not enough to lean on alone.

Net: the fast-moving, market-repricing drivers (labor, inflation) have both lost conviction and no longer clearly point toward lower yields — they're closer to neutral/flat now than a month ago. Term premium's own uptick adds a modest independent tilt higher. This undercuts the case for staying duration-long. I'm cutting the position size meaningfully rather than flipping outright, since no driver has cleanly reversed to "up" with strong conviction — this is a fade toward neutral, not a directional flip.

discounted.why: High conviction but slow-moving, well-anticipated QT pace; limited marginal repricing power.
Weak, range-bound, low-conviction tick; not a strong level signal on its own.

Term premium uptick could be noise given negative medium-term trend and missing supply/risk-sentiment context.
Panel disagreement between labor/inflation (softening) and term premium (rising) weakens conviction in any single direction.

trade.rationale: Cut prior duration-long position materially: labor and inflation softened their downward-yield conviction while term premium ticked up, eroding the case for staying long duration but not flipping it.

The two-observation dip (0.19→0.20→0.15) could be noise or partial retracement, but the broader multi-horizon momentum and the policy pivot toward cuts both argue for the steepening trend resuming rather than reversing. The 10s30s at +0.33 also shows a normal, healthily positive long-end slope, consistent with an un-inverted curve environment. My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction terms: headline y/y did fall further (to 2.43%), continuing the down trend I had discounted. More importantly, core PCE y/y ticked UP for the second straight month (2.75 → 2.81 → 2.87), a genuine reversal in momentum after months of steady decline, and core_yoy_change_3m is now +0.10pp (accelerating), not decelerating. Policy language also shifted to "somewhat elevated" (softer than "elevated") and "some further progress" (downgraded from "modest further progress" — actually an upgrade in the disinflation narrative, but core PCE's turn up complicates that. Given headline's recent monthly firming (0.21% vs 0.05% two months prior) and core PCE's clear upward inflection, I lean toward headline CPI ticking up or flat next month rather than continuing its glide down.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print back below 0.05% or renewed core PCE y/y decline below 2.75% would restore the down/flat disinflation call.

Given the flattening 3m trend, the tick-down from the recent high, and range position still elevated at 0.80, I read this as a pause/stabilization rather than a resumption of tightening or a fresh loosening leg. I lean flat with a slight upward bias given the 6/12m trend context, but conviction is low given the single-month reversal.

arbitrated.notes:
Since last meeting, the panel has shifted meaningfully against my prior modest yields-down tilt. Term premium flipped from "up" to "flat" (conviction only 0.25) — the analyst admits the prior thesis was too confident, with dgs10 near the bottom of its 12m range and negative 63d/252d momentum still dominant despite a small recent uptick. That's a live, near-term read and it's now neutral-to-soft, not supportive of higher yields. Financial conditions flipped hard from "up" to "down" (0.55 conviction) — NFCI has eased monotonically for six straight readings, near 52w loose extreme. Looser financial conditions are disinflationary/growth-supportive at the margin and tend to associate with lower term premium and richer duration, reinforcing the yields-down case. Inflation is the one driver that turned harder: core PCE y/y has now risen two straight months (2.75→2.81→2.87) with accelerating 3m change, a genuine reversal, though headline CPI y/y hit a new 12m low. This is the most trade-relevant near-term driver since it feeds directly into the Fed's reaction function, and it argues modestly for higher yields. Labor tightness ticked down (unemployment 4.20→4.10) validating a stabilization/flat read with low conviction — not a strong lever either way. Balance sheet continues runoff as expected (down, 0.68 conviction) — but this is slow-moving, well-anticipated QT continuation, already priced; I discount its yields-up push accordingly. Curve slope is a shape signal I don't use for level.

Netting these: term premium's own downgrade and financial conditions' clear loosening outweigh the modest inflation uptick, especially since inflation's own signal is mixed (headline down, core up) and low-conviction on my synthesis. The panel is genuinely more mixed than a month ago, but the term-premium retreat plus FC easing tilts me to hold a very light yields-down bias rather than flip to up. I trim conviction slightly given the increased disagreement.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel is largely a continuation of last month's picture, not a reversal, but the balance of live signals has shifted modestly toward yields-up versus my prior stance. Balance sheet runoff (down, 0.72, fresher and even slightly accelerating vs 13w pace) remains a genuine structural support for higher long-end yields via reduced Fed demand/duration absorption — this is the most concrete, non-stale read on the panel. Inflation (up, 0.45, only 16 days stale) shows two consecutive months of re-accelerating headline MoM and a real, if marginal, uptick in core PCE — this is the near-term repricing driver per the framework and it has moved against my prior "up but modest" stance, now firmer. Labor tightness has stabilized (flat, 0.35) — unemployment plateaued at 4.10 after peaking at 4.20, removing the loosening impulse that supported my prior yields-down lean; this driver now reads neutral-to-slightly-tightening, which is itself a mild hawkish/yields-up shift versus last time. Financial conditions continue to ease (down, 0.60) — a slow, well-anticipated driver, already priced, and per the framework I downweight its confident-but-slow signal; it still argues for accommodative conditions capping any big yields-up move, but far less than its conviction score alone would suggest. Term premium is flat/plateauing with a mild upward lean, but explicitly lacks balance-sheet and FC context — I lean on the fresher balance-sheet and inflation reads to fill that gap rather than take term_premium's own flat call at face value.

Net: the previously bearish-yields tilt has lost its footing. Labor loosening reversed, inflation re-accelerated, and balance-sheet runoff continues unabated — three of the more market-moving, near-term drivers now point modestly toward higher yields, while only the slow-moving, largely-priced FC easing still argues the other way. This warrants a small flip from short-duration-favoring (yields down) toward a modest yields-up lean, not a large repositioning given the panel's own internal caution (curve_slope stalling, term_premium only mildly biased, FC still easing).

arbitrated.notes: Last meeting I ran a modest long-duration (yields-up-lean was implicit? no — actually my legs were positive weight meaning I was positioned for yields up) view built mainly on inflation reaccelerating and labor tightening slightly, offset by clearly easing financial conditions and shrinking balance sheet. This meeting's panel largely confirms the inflation and labor calls but adds a fresh, direct read on term_premium (10y yield itself) that has now turned down with real momentum (4.41→4.18, 21d change -0.10), plus inflation_expectations (breakevens) turning down too. That's a live, non-stale cross-read directly on the level I'm judging, and it's telling me the long end has actually been rallying, not selling off, despite the inflation/labor upticks. Financial conditions keep easing (fresh 52w low) and balance sheet runoff keeps accelerating — these are largely priced, slow-moving forces, so I downweight them per instructions despite their high conviction. Inflation (CPI/PCE reaccelerating, conviction 0.55) and labor tightening (unemployment at cycle high, loosening labor market — which is disinflationary/dovish for policy despite "tightness" naming) are the near-term repricing drivers, but they point in different directions on net: inflation up argues yields up, but labor loosening (unemployment rising) argues yields down via easier Fed. Term premium's actual yield-level decline is the most direct, freshest evidence of where the long end is actually trading, and it corroborates the labor-loosening/dovish read rather than the inflation-up read. Given the panel is split and the freshest, most rate-relevant driver (term_premium, plus breakevens) point toward lower yields, I should scale back my prior long-duration lean rather than hold it fully. I'll shrink the position toward flat/small down-tilt rather than reverse hard, since inflation still has decent conviction and could reassert.

So both falsifiers failed to trigger and momentum continued building. Headline y/y has now risen for three straight months off the 2.43% trough, a cumulative +0.44pp, and the 3m-annualized headline pace (3.70%) sits well above both the y/y print and target, signaling further near-term upward pressure. Core 3m-annualized (2.74%) is more modest but still running above the y/y level, and core distance from target remains basically stuck near 1pp with no genuine progress over the last several prints (2.75→2.98 bouncing, not falling). The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is roughly flat (-0.01pp), meaning the trend isn't decelerating either. Policy language downgraded from "further progress" to just "progress," consistent with the data-driven stalling in disinflation. With headline mom averaging 0.30% for three months (an annualized rate near 3.6%), another firm print is likely, keeping y/y elevated or edging up further. I maintain an up call, slightly higher conviction given confirmed momentum.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.15%, or core PCE y/y falling back under 2.85%, would flip this toward flat.

Over the last four months the rate has oscillated in a narrow 4.10-4.20 band (4.10, 4.20, 4.10, 4.20, 4.10), showing stabilization rather than continued loosening. The 3m and 6m changes are both flat (+0.00 pp), indicating no fresh momentum in either direction. However, the 12m change is still +0.30 pp, and the rate remains 0.40 pp above its 12-month low with a range position of 0.80 (near the top of the past year's range) and a Sahm-gap of 0.43 pp — close to the 0.50 threshold associated with genuine turning points, so the level is not fully reassuring. Policy language now explicitly says conditions have "generally eased" since earlier in the year, a firmer acknowledgment of loosening than the prior "moved up but remains low" phrasing, suggesting officials see the higher-rate regime as durable, not just noise. On balance, the market looks like it has plateaued at a modestly higher, looser level rather than actively deteriorating further right now. I'll call this closer to flat than up, but keep some upward lean given the elevated Sahm-gap and eased characterization.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 4.0% would confirm re-tightening; a fresh push above 4.2% would confirm renewed loosening and revive the up call.

arbitrated.notes: This panel is a meaningful departure from last meeting's setup, and the evidence now points net higher on yields rather than the marginal +0.05/+0.05 I was carrying on weak conviction. Two changes matter most: term_premium flipped from down(-0.40) to up(+0.55) with a clean, accelerating multi-window uptrend in 10y (21d/63d/252d all positive, near 12-month highs), and inflation continues to run hot and confirmed (headline CPI accelerating three straight months, core PCE ticking up, momentum not decelerating). These are the two drivers that actually reprice the level: term_premium is the direct read on the long end, and inflation is the live, near-term repricing driver per the instructions. Labor_tightness is genuinely flat now (was +0.50, now 0.30 conviction flat) — the loosening stalled, removing a disinflationary labor argument but not adding a strong signal either way. Balance_sheet (-0.65) and financial_conditions (-0.45) both continue their slow easing/runoff trends, but these are slow-moving, well-anticipated drivers already priced in — balance_sheet runoff is yields-supportive (less demand-side support, i.e., pushes yields up) while easier financial conditions are mildly yields-supportive too via risk appetite, but neither should dominate the live inflation/term-premium signal. The panel flags term_premium is missing balance_sheet and financial_conditions context, but both of those actually reinforce the up-yield read (runoff = supply pressure up, easier conditions = no flight-to-quality reversal), so the gap doesn't undercut the read — it's corroborating. Given term_premium's clear reversal plus inflation's confirmed reacceleration, my own stated falsifier from last meeting (10y above 4.35, breakevens above 2.30, CPI confirming reacceleration) has been met. I'm resizing modestly higher on conviction and shifting the position to a clearer, still-modest yields-up lean, weighted more on the long end given term_premium is the more direct duration signal.
