This meeting largely confirms the prior read rather than overturning it. Inflation remains the strongest, freshest, and most trade-relevant driver: headline CPI y/y up to 2.99, core PCE also up, 3m annualized momentum running hot (+4.29%), and the analyst's own flip conditions (mom<0.15%, core PCE<2.85%) are nowhere close to triggering. Inflation expectations corroborate this with breakevens near 12m highs, though that series is slower-moving and largely already priced. Term premium also confirms: 10y near cycle highs, range_position 0.82, 63d/252d trends still up, falsifier (sub-4.45 with 30s10s flattening) not triggered — this is a live, non-stale read that matters for the long end. Labor tightness is closer to neutral: unemployment ticked down to 4.00%, a mild re-tightening signal, but magnitude is small and single-month — I'll nudge this slightly negative (tighter labor) rather than flat, since falling unemployment is the tightening direction, but keep conviction low given noise.

Balance sheet and financial conditions are both continuing, slow-moving, well-anticipated trends (runoff continuing, NFCI grinding lower) — directionally unchanged from last time but low marginal information value for repricing yields since markets have had a full quarter to price them in. I keep the balance-sheet call (assets down = supply/duration effect keeps upward pressure on yields) but weight it lightly in the trade. Financial conditions easing (down) is modestly yield-supportive on the dovish side but again glacial and priced.

Net: nothing here flips the prior call. Inflation and term premium — the two live, fast-moving, market-relevant reads — both continue to point toward higher yields, and my stated falsifiers didn't trigger. I hold the existing long-duration-yields position (steepener-flavored, both legs same sign) unchanged, perhaps trimming conviction slightly given panel-wide moderate (not high) convictions and a slight softening in term premium's 21d momentum.

arbitrated.notes:
My prior long-duration-favors-higher-yields stance (DGS10 +0.60, DGS2 +0.30) was built on inflation reaccelerating (+0.62), tightening financial conditions, and rising term premium. This meeting's panel largely reverses that setup. Inflation flipped from up to down (0.45 conviction) with both my own stated falsifiers hit — headline mom decelerated sharply and core PCE breached 2.85 — this is the freshest, most decision-relevant driver and it moved hard against my prior read. Term premium also flipped from up to down (0.45): the 10y grind from 4.47 to 4.24 breached my own 4.45 trigger, a clean, fresh signal on the instrument I actually trade. Labor tightness ticked up in the unemployment print (looser labor, consistent with lower yields) but the analyst still calls it range-bound noise — weak, low-conviction signal, roughly neutral-to-mild-easing for yields. Financial conditions turned from easing to a mild tightening lean, but conviction is modest (0.40) and magnitude tiny; this is a slow-moving, largely-priced driver and I downweight it per instructions. Balance sheet continues shrinking on trend (0.75 conviction) — real and structural, pushes yields up, but it's a well-anticipated, slow-moving force fully priced into the term-premium/QT narrative already, so I don't let it dominate against two fresher, harder-hitting reversals in inflation and term premium.

Net: the two most trade-relevant, freshest drivers (inflation deceleration, term premium decline) both point toward lower yields, directly falsifying my prior "yields higher" thesis. I flip the position to a modest duration-long (yields lower) stance, sized down given continued QT and some financial-conditions tightening as partial offsets, and given genuine disagreement/noise in labor and breakevens.

discounted.why: Slow-moving, small-magnitude reversal off lows; largely priced, low trade relevance for level view.
Analyst itself frames the move as range-bound noise, not a trend break.
Well-anticipated, gradual QT continuation; structural but fully priced, not a fresh repricing catalyst.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI mom rebound above ~0.35% or core PCE back above 2.95%, combined with the 10y reversing back above 4.40 and 30s10s steepening, would flip this back toward higher yields.

risks.text: Fed could hold hawkish framing despite softer inflation prints, capping the yield decline.
Single-month inflation and term-premium moves could prove noisy and reverse next print.
Continued QT (balance sheet shrinkage) is a structural offset that could reassert upward pressure on yields.
Financial conditions tightening lean, if it extends, could pressure yields higher against this trade.

trade.rationale: Fresh reversal in both inflation and term premium (the two most trade-relevant, live drivers) breached their own falsifier thresholds, flipping the level view toward lower yields; sized down given QT offset and mixed labor/financial-conditions signals.

  Last release: headline CPI y/y fell sharply to 2.38% from 2.80%, and the monthly print was just 0.03% m/m — well below my falsifier threshold of 0.35%, so the prior "down" call was validated, not falsified. That said, core PCE y/y actually rose to 2.97% from 2.78%, back above my other falsifier level (2.95%), which is a genuine cross-current. Core PCE has been oscillating in a tight 2.75-3.12% band with no clear trend (3m annualized core running hot at 3.87%), while headline's 3m annualized is a much cooler 2.78% and the y/y series just hit a fresh 12-month low. The divergence is real: headline is being flattered by favorable base effects/energy, while core — the better persistence signal — shows no decisive progress and if anything ticked up. Given the single very low 0.03% mom print, some mean-reversion in the next headline y/y reading is likely (base effects won't repeat, and momentum had been running ~0.22-0.23% for months prior). I weight core's stickiness as a caution against extrapolating the drop, but for the specific headline y/y horizon, a bounce off an unusually soft month is the more probable near-term move.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print again below ~0.10% or core PCE y/y falling back under 2.85% would confirm continued disinflation, not a bounce.

The range position sits at the top of its 12-month band (1.00) and the Sahm-style gap (+0.23pp) is still well below the ~0.5pp threshold that typically signals a genuine recessionary turn. Policy language has shifted constructively, now describing conditions as "stabilized at a low level" and "solid," a clear softening from the prior "generally eased" language — corroborating the flattening in the hard data. Given the rate is unchanged month-over-month, the pace of increase has decelerated to near-zero over the past couple of readings, and the qualitative language reinforces stabilization, I read this as a flat labor market at a slightly elevated but not deteriorating level. Risk is that a print above 4.2% would flip this to a genuine loosening signal, but that hasn't happened yet.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.2% (new 12-month high) would confirm renewed loosening; a drop back toward 4.0% would confirm stabilization/tightening.

The two drivers that actually move the near-term rate axis are inflation and labor. Inflation now shows a genuine cross-current: headline CPI y/y is at a fresh low and validated the down call, but core PCE ticked back above the analyst's own 2.95 falsifier level, and the analyst itself flags likely mean-reversion higher in headline next print. That's a live signal turning less disinflationary than last month — I read inflation as leaning up now, a partial reversal of my prior -0.45 stance. Labor tightness stayed flat at 4.20%, no new information to move yields either way — stalled at the ceiling, unrate flat, so effectively neutral, slightly less unrate-loosening than last month's near-flat lean into tightening data. Term premium is still net down but momentum has visibly stalled (21d flat vs 63d down), and it faces an unaddressed gap versus balance-sheet detail — the slowing QT taper is bullish for term premium but the analyst hasn't fully priced the new $5B cap's magnitude. arbitrated.notes: Relative to last meeting, the trade-relevant near-term drivers (inflation, labor) have moved modestly further toward disinflation/no-tightening, while the slower, largely-priced drivers (balance sheet, financial conditions, term premium) are mixed but mostly consistent with a soft-to-flat yield backdrop. Inflation: headline CPI y/y actually fell to a 12-month low and core PCE ticked down too, despite a hot 3m-annualized core print; net call is flat with a slight softening tilt versus my prior "up" call, which was wrong. Labor: unemployment plateaued at a 12-month high (4.20%), i.e., labor is loosening (not tightening) but not accelerating — a flat/no-tightening read, mildly yield-negative at the margin versus a hawkish alternative. These two are the drivers that actually reprice yields near-term, and both argue for no fresh upward inflation/labor pressure — supportive of yields staying contained or drifting down slightly, reversing none of my prior thesis. Term premium continues its multi-month decline (10y down to 4.17, still below my 4.40 flip threshold), directly supportive of lower yields and confirming rather than contradicting the carried position. Balance sheet: runoff pace is sharply decelerating (Treasury cap cut 80%), a slower drift down rather than reversal — this is mildly hawkish for term premium (less disinflationary supply relief) but is a slow, well-anticipated mechanical driver, so I keep its weight low. Financial conditions continue tightening modestly, near top of 52-week range, a genuine tightening impulse but decelerating and already largely priced. Curve slope analyst is out of scope (curve pod). Net: no driver has "turned" against my carried short-duration-yield view; if anything inflation and labor confirm it further. I hold the position essentially unchanged, perhaps trimming size slightly given financial conditions' tightening drag and the deceleration in term premium's own downward momentum.

However, the policy language shows a material deceleration ahead: effective [date] the Treasury redemption cap drops from $25B/month to $5B/month, while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B. This is a substantial slowing of the pace of QT (potential total cap runoff falls from up to $60B/month to as low as $40B/month, though actual paydowns are often below caps). The direction remains runoff (down) — assets should still decline into month-end — but the recent 4-week and 13-week pace (~$9-30B/month) already looks close to or even below the new combined cap ceiling, so continued decline is likely but at a decidedly slower rate than the trailing 52-week pace (-611,075 $M) would suggest. I maintain a "down" call for next month-end, but with reduced conviction given the explicit policy shift toward a much slower runoff pace, and note Waller's dissent shows the taper itself was contested, underscoring this is a genuine regime change, not just noise.
Would change its mind if: A weekly rise in fed_assets, or evidence that actual Treasury+MBS runoff has flattened to near-zero under the new $5B/$35B caps.

arbitrated.notes:
Last meeting I ran a modest bull-flattener-ish duration long (short DGS10 more than DGS2, both negative) on the back of a dovish term-premium call, easing-tilted financial conditions read, and QT-reduction thesis. This meeting's panel materially undercuts that: term_premium itself now reports "flat" with the analyst explicitly reversing direction versus a month ago (dgs10_change_21d +0.24, change_63d +0.17 — a net RISE, not a decline), and flags it was never handed the balance_sheet or financial_conditions readings that used to justify my down-tilt. Financial conditions flipped to "down" (easing) with decent conviction (0.45) — that is a genuine repricing, since looser conditions is consistent with somewhat lower term premium/duration demand pressure, supporting yields not rising sharply, but it is a slow-moving, largely-priced driver and shouldn't dominate. Balance sheet remains "down" (runoff continues) but conviction fell and the analyst flags a real regime shift — QT is decelerating (redemption cap $25B→$5B), which is a supply-relief force pushing against higher term premium, i.e. modestly yield-supportive on the down side. But this is slow and already partly priced.

The near-term repricing drivers — inflation (flat/softening, cooling core PCE, low mom) and labor_tightness (up to 4.30%, fresh 12m high, loosening labor market) — both point toward disinflation and labor slack, which is the classic combination for lower, not higher, yields. These are the drivers that actually move the front end and reaction function pricing. Given term_premium's own directional call reversed to flat/up-leaning while the two most market-moving, live drivers (inflation cooling, labor loosening) still argue for lower yields, I keep a modest duration-long bias but with reduced size/conviction versus last meeting, since term_premium — the most levered driver for the long end — no longer confirms the easing direction it previously did.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has shifted meaningfully since last meeting, and the shift is toward higher, not lower, yields relative to my prior small-short-duration stance. Term premium reversed from down (-0.40) to up (+0.35) — the analyst explicitly says its own falsifier (rebound above 4.35 with 30s10s steepening) has fired, and this is a fresh, self-acknowledged flip on a driver that moves the long end directly via supply/duration premium. That's the single most decision-relevant change this meeting. Labor tightness continues to firm (unemployment drifting to 4.30%, 12m high) — a loosening labor market, which is bond-bullish for the front end, though momentum is modest and this call is already 23 days old (semi-stale). Inflation is up but decelerating in the 3m-annualized sense — mild, aging (16 days), and equivocal on core PCE; low weight. Balance sheet and financial conditions both continue their "down" trend (QT continuing, NFCI at 52w lows) but both are slow-moving, well-anticipated drivers already priced into the curve — I discount their fresh conviction accordingly despite decent conviction scores, per the mandate to underweight confident calls on slow variables. Curve slope isn't mine to trade but its flattening (short rates rising faster than long) is a corroborating cross-read: consistent with tightening-side pressure on front end more than a broad bull move.

Net: the previously bond-bullish setup (easing FC, falling term premium, disinflation lean) has partially reversed — term premium up is the freshest, most consequential move, partially offset by continued labor loosening. This tilts the net level view from modestly-long-duration to roughly neutral-to-modestly-short, a meaningful update from last meeting's small long position. I'm flipping the sign on my duration bet, but keeping size very small given the panel is genuinely split (inflation up but decelerating, labor loosening but slow, term premium up but tight-range, FC/BS still easing but slow-moving) — disagreement here argues for downsizing rather than a confident directional bet.

arbitrated.notes:
The picture has shifted meaningfully from last meeting and my prior modest long-yield tilt (+0.12/+0.08, conviction 0.30) needs to be pared back, though not fully reversed.

The two drivers that actually move the level near-term — inflation and labor tightness — still point the same direction as before, but with different force. Headline CPI momentum is genuinely accelerating (0.55 conviction, fresh 16-day read, 3m annualized running hot above y/y), and that is a real repricing input: it argues for higher, not lower, yields. Labor tightness, however, has downgraded to a plateau rather than a continued climb — unemployment held at 4.30% rather than drifting further, conviction fell to 0.30, and the analyst's own falsifier (a hold/print back at 4.10) has not fired but the deterioration has clearly decelerated. That's a weaker tightening impulse than last month.

Term premium flipped hard: last meeting it screamed "up" (0.50 conviction); now it's "down" (0.35) with 21d and 63d momentum both negative and the explicit acknowledgment that the prior call was wrong. This is the freshest, most direct read on the long end and it has reversed. It also flags dependencies on financial_conditions and inflation_expectations it wasn't given — but financial conditions itself is now flat/stalled (easing has plateaued, not continuing), so it doesn't resolve the ambiguity in term premium's favor either way; I treat this as a wash, mildly reinforcing "no fresh easing impulse."

Balance sheet continues its slow, monotonic, well-anticipated runoff — a background upward force on term premium/yields, but it's the slowest-moving, most priced-in driver here and shouldn't dominate.

Net: an upward inflation impulse now fighting a reversed term-premium signal and a decelerating labor-tightening signal. The composite case for higher yields has weakened materially versus last meeting. I'm cutting my long-duration-yield lean roughly in half rather than flipping outright, since inflation's acceleration is real and CPI is the freshest, highest-impact print in the panel.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has shifted modestly against my prior mild-up-yield tilt, and I should size down rather than hold at the same conviction. Inflation remains the strongest live signal: CPI confirmed acceleration (headline y/y to 3.02%, core PCE to 2.91%, 3m annualized running hot), and this is the most trade-relevant driver since it feeds directly into the near-term reaction function. That alone argues for continued upward pressure on nominal yields. But labor_tightness also strengthened its "up" call (unemployment rising to 4.40%, 12-month high, accelerating loosening) — a looser labor market is disinflationary and dovish for policy, pulling against the inflation signal on the rate axis. These two live, high-quality reads now point in opposite directions for yields, which is a genuine widening of panel disagreement versus last meeting, when inflation and labor both leaned toward higher yields via tightness/persistence.

Term_premium (proxying 10y level itself) is flat/stalling — no confirmation of the down break I'd need to flip bearish-yields, but also no acceleration higher. Balance_sheet flipped from confidently down to flat, weakening the QT-tightening-financial-conditions channel that had supported higher term premium; this is a slow, largely-priced driver anyway so I weight it lightly. Financial_conditions is pinned flat, offering no impulse. Curve_slope's flattening-stall doesn't speak to level.

Net: the fresh CPI print is the single most decisive piece of new evidence and it argues for keeping a slight long-yield (higher rates) lean, but the labor deterioration and stalling term-premium/balance-sheet readings materially dilute conviction versus last meeting. I hold the same direction but shrink size, given rising internal disagreement and no driver decisively confirming a bigger move.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has drifted up over the last three prints (3.99→4.08→4.11→4.11), pushing it to the top of its recent trading band and roughly flat over the past week but modestly higher on the month. It still sits well below the 12-month high (4.79) and range position (0.17) shows it remains in the lower third of the yearly range, so this is not a breakout — more a stabilization near the upper end of a recent narrow band after months of decline (63d and 252d changes still negative). The 30s10s slope at 0.56pp is elevated versus my prior threshold of concern (0.65) but has been drifting up, suggesting some long-end-specific richness building. Policy language changed materially: the Fed announced it will conclude balance-sheet runoff, which removes a technical source of upward pressure on long-end supply/duration that had been previously cited. That is a mild negative for term premium looking forward, working against the recent modest uptick. My previous flat call was directionally fine — level barely moved net (change_21d only -0.01). Given the tension between recent modest upward drift and the balance-sheet language shift removing a upward driver, I lean flat-to-slightly-up but without strong conviction.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 4.20 with 30s10s steepening past 0.65 would confirm renewed upward pressure; a move below 3.95 would confirm downside continuation.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Holding close to my prior read but trimming size slightly. The two drivers that actually move yields on a near-term repricing basis — inflation and labor tightness — are both still calling "up," but both are now stale (46 and 53 days old respectively) with no fresher CPI/jobs print to confirm or refute. My stated flip triggers (CPI m/m <0.15%, unemployment back to ≤4.20%) have not been tested with new data, so I can't say the inflationary/loosening-labor impulse has reversed, but I also can't lean harder on stale reads than I did last time. Term premium and financial conditions are flat with low-to-moderate conviction and are largely priced/slow-moving — financial conditions is pinned at multi-month lows with zero momentum, term premium shows a modest recent uptick offset by the QT-conclusion announcement removing a technical upward driver. Balance sheet flips notably: the Fed's explicit language ending QT is a real regime change, but this is a slow-moving, well-anticipated driver for the level of yields, and if anything argues for a touch of downward pressure on term premium/long-end supply concerns, partially offsetting the inflation/labor upward pull rather than reinforcing it. Curve_slope is a curve-shape signal and out of scope; inflation_expectations (breakevens) is a real/nominal split question and out of scope for this level call, though its "flat, no breakout" finding is consistent with no acceleration in inflation risk premium. Net: the fundamental upward tilt from inflation and labor persists but is unconfirmed and now overlaid by a genuine ballast (QT conclusion) working the other way. This argues for keeping the modest long position but not adding to it — a case for holding size, not for conviction growth.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.55) was wrong: fed_assets fell further, from 6,552,419 down to... actually the latest print (6,552,419) is the newest and lowest in the series, continuing a clear downtrend. The 4-week change is -34,615M and 13-week change is -50,965M, both negative, and the series has been grinding lower almost every observation for the past several months, now 345,066M below the 52-week high. This is runoff, not expansion, and it was still running at the time of the latest data point despite the new statement language announcing an end to reduction "on [date] " — a forward-looking commitment whose effective date is not yet reflected in the actual asset data shown. The policy language changed to say QT will conclude, which is the key reason to expect the pace of decline to flatten going forward, but the measured data through the last print still shows active, uninterrupted runoff with no sign of stabilization yet. Since the graded measurement is total assets and recent pace dominates, and the announcement's effective date is unknown relative to month-end, I weight the still-declining actual data more heavily this month while acknowledging the language points toward an imminent halt.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print at or above the prior level (flat/positive change) confirming the runoff has actually stopped per the new statement.

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.30) anticipated a push above 0.55pp with positive 21d momentum confirming steepening. The latest prints show slope ticking up to 0.58 twice before settling back to 0.55 in the last two observations — so the steepening impulse partially materialized then faded, landing flat versus the prior reading. The 21d change is a modest +0.03pp (essentially flat), while the 63d change is -0.10pp, showing a recent flattening drift over the medium term that has stalled. The 252d change of +0.51pp confirms the dominant one-year trend has been steepening from near-zero, and range position at 0.82 (near the 12m high of 0.67) shows the curve remains historically un-inverted relative to its own recent history. The Fed's continued rate cuts (25bp, following another 25bp) support front-end anchoring while removing some flattening pressure, but the curve has plateaued at 0.55 for two straight readings after a brief push to 0.58, suggesting consolidation rather than a fresh trend. With mixed short-term momentum (up 21d, down 63d) and the level stalling just off its highs, this looks like sideways drift rather than a resumption of steepening or a return to flattening. I revise down conviction on "up" given the failed follow-through above 0.58.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 0.58pp sustained for multiple readings would confirm renewed steepening; a drop below 0.50pp would confirm flattening resuming.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has ticked up marginally from -0.52 to -0.50 over the recent window, a very small +0.01 to +0.02 index move over 4 and 13 weeks. This is consistent with my prior flat call being roughly correct - the series has been essentially pinned near -0.52 for most of the window with only the last observation nudging higher. Conditions remain deeply loose relative to the zero average (distance from zero of -0.50), well below the historical mean, and the 52-week range (-0.52 to -038) shows we're still near the loose end (range position 0.14). The tiny uptick is within noise for an index that moved by hundredths over 13 observations, and it hasn't breached my stated reversal threshold of -0.45. There's no policy language pointing to a shift in financial conditions. I don't see enough momentum here to call a genuine tightening turn - this still looks like a flat, low-volatility regime with a marginal drift toward tightening that isn't yet confirmed. I'll shade my conviction slightly down from pure flat neutrality given the consistent (if tiny) uptick across both 4-week and 13-week windows, which is a mild lean toward gradual tightening rather than pure stasis.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.45 with flat-to-positive 4-week change confirms tightening; a fresh break below -0.52 signals renewed easing.

The series has drifted from 2.28 to 2.23 over the last ten readings, with be_change_21d at -0.05pp and be_change_63d at -0.20pp, both negative and consistent in direction. The 252d change of -0.11pp confirms this is not just short-term noise but part of a longer grind lower. The range position of 0.21 (near the 12m low of 2.17) further shows breakevens sitting in the bottom quintile of their annual range, having compressed from a high of 2.46. That said, the last three readings (2.22, 2.23, 2.23) show the decline losing momentum and possibly stabilizing just under 2.25 — this is a shallow drift, not a sharp break. Distance from the 2% target remains a still-material +0.23pp, so anchoring is intact but expectations continue to soften. Given deceleration in the pace of decline but a clear established lower trend versus a month ago, I lean toward continued mild softening rather than reversal.

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline printed 2.99% y/y (down slightly from 3.02%), with m/m of 0.25% — essentially matching the recent 3m average (0.30%) and not falling below my 0.15% threshold, though it did tick just under the 2.9% y/y trigger... actually it landed at 2.99%, right at my watch level. My prior "up" call was roughly correct in direction (headline had been climbing from 2.33 to 3.02 over the year) but the latest print shows the ascent stalling rather than continuing — a marginal decline, not a fresh high. Headline 3m annualized (3.64%) still runs hotter than the y/y rate, suggesting some near-term upward pressure remains in the pipeline. However, core PCE continues to decelerate (2.75%, down from 2.99% oldest, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.11pp and core_3m_annualized at a tame 2.60%), indicating underlying persistence is easing even as headline stays elevated near the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.95). The gap between core and headline (-0.23pp) reflects energy/food volatility inflating headline. Given headline is near cycle highs but momentum (mom_gap_vs_outgoing +0.01) is flat rather than accelerating, and core is softening, I see the headline y/y as more likely to hold flat-to-slightly-down next month rather than push to new highs.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or a jump in headline y/y above 3.1% would signal renewed acceleration beyond the current plateau.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 4.50% from 4.40%, extending the up-trend I flagged last time and confirming the "up" call rather than the stabilization scenario. Unemployment is now at its 12-month high, sitting exactly at the top of the range (range_position=1.00), with the rate up 0.4pp over 12 months and 0.2pp over just the last 3 months. That 3-month pace, if annualized, is a meaningfully faster deterioration than the 12-month trend, consistent with a loosening labor market rather than noise around a flat level. The Sahm-style gap (current minus 12m low) is 0.50pp — not yet at the classic 0.50 trigger threshold in a stricter reading, but close enough to be notable, especially given the monotonic recent rise (4.30, 4.30, 4.40, 4.50) rather than a choppy pattern. Policy language changes reinforce this: officials now describe unemployment risks as having "risen" (past tense, more definitive) versus "risen" as an ongoing assessment previously, and job gains "slowed this year" is a firmer, more historical framing. This supports continued upward drift rather than reversal. Level is now 0.5pp above my 4% natural-rate anchor, and combined with the accelerating recent pace, I lean toward another modest increase or at least no reversion in the next print.
Would change its mind if: A print back at/below 4.30% would signal stabilization; a print above 4.60% would confirm sharp acceleration and raise conviction further.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

The 63-day and 252-day changes are both meaningfully negative (-0.22pp, -0.39pp), confirming a sustained downtrend, though the most recent 21-day change is slightly positive (+0.03pp), suggesting the decline has stalled and yields have ticked up modestly off the lows in the very latest readings (4.00→4.02). The 30s10s slope is 0.65pp, right at my prior "steepening" trigger level, but this reflects long-end curve shape rather than a confirmed breakout in the 10-year itself. The policy language shift - ending balance-sheet runoff - removes a source of upward pressure on term premium (less net Treasury supply absorption needed from private market), which is consistent with the recent low yields and argues against a resumption of the uptrend. On balance, the dominant signal is a yield sitting at 12-month lows with a supportive policy shift, but the tiny recent uptick tempers conviction that the down-move continues cleanly into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A clean break above 4.15 with 30s10s steepening further past 0.70 would signal renewed upward term-premium pressure; a fresh low below 3.95 confirms continuation lower.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has shifted materially against my prior modestly-long-yields carry. Labor tightness remains the most trade-relevant driver and it strengthened: unemployment at 4.50%, a 12-month high, monotonic uptrend, Sahm-style gap near trigger — this is a live, repricing-relevant signal and it argues unemployment keeps rising, i.e., labor loosens further, which is disinflationary/dovish for yields (down pressure). Inflation itself is flat-to-decelerating: headline stalled at 2.99%, core PCE cooling, and inflation_expectations (breakevens) confirms a real, multi-week grind lower, near 12-month lows. That is two independent, live reads (headline plateau + breakeven decay) both pointing to easing inflation pressure — a clear reversal from last meeting's +0.50 inflation conviction. Term premium/10y itself is sitting at 12-month lows (4.02), with only a tentative stabilization tick, and financial conditions are flat/marginally tightening (low signal value, priced). Balance sheet: still runoff in the data, but forward language points to conclusion — mixed, stale-ish policy-language vs backward-looking data, so I discount its independent yield impact given the term-premium desk already flags the same ambiguity.

Net: the near-term reaction-function drivers (labor loosening further, inflation stalling/breakevens declining) have flipped from mild upward pressure to mild downward pressure on yields, while slow drivers (balance sheet, financial conditions) are ambiguous/priced. My prior long-duration-lite carry (+0.05/+0.03) is no longer supported — the fresh CPI/labor evidence I set as my own falsifier has essentially arrived (unemployment marching toward and headline inflation stalling, breakevens breaking down). I flip to a small short-duration-lite (lower yields) position, sized modestly given continued panel disagreement (curve_slope flat, term_premium tentative stabilization).

  The last several weekly prints show total assets rising sharply: 6,535,781 → 6,539,303 → 6,556,861 → 6,581,231, a clear turn off the trough. The 4-week change is now +28,812$M, positive, reversing the prior downtrend, even though the 13-week and 52-week changes remain negative (-27,164$M and -304,732$M respectively) reflecting the tail of the runoff period. My prior call of "down" was wrong for this most recent stretch — the weekly data confirms the trigger I specified (a print at/above prior levels, in fact a multi-week rising streak). This aligns with the policy language shift: the Committee has moved from "concluding reduction of holdings" to explicitly stating it will "initiate purchases of shorter-term Treasury securities as needed to maintain an ample supply of reserves on an ongoing basis." This is a clear statement that QT has ended and modest reserve-management purchases (effectively QE-lite) are beginning. Given both the measurement turn and the explicit policy language change, I expect total assets to be higher at next month-end, continuing the recent rebound, though the pace is likely to be modest/gradual rather than a large-scale expansion given the "as needed" framing.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print falling back below ~6,556,861$M (reversing the recent rebound) would undercut the expansion read.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call of "flat" was wrong: the spread has continued to steepen, moving from 0.67 to 0.71pp over the last ten readings, with the 21d and 63d changes both solidly positive (+0.16, +0.19pp) and the 252d change even larger (+0.43pp), confirming a durable multi-month steepening trend rather than noise. The spread now sits at 0.96 on the 12-month range position — essentially at its highs for the year — having moved well off its 12-month low of 0.20pp. That said, the very latest print (0.71) follows a small pullback sequence (0.73→0.70→0.68→0.68→0.67→0.69→0.71), so there's some choppiness within the uptrend rather than a clean monotonic climb. Policy language shows continued rate cuts (Fed lowering the funds rate range again) with an added phrase emphasizing "extent and timing" of further adjustments — consistent with an easing cycle that typically supports front-end anchoring and further curve steepening as long-end yields don't fall as fast. The 10s30s slope at 0.66pp, roughly in line with 2s10s, suggests the long end isn't independently inverting the picture. Given the trend strength and level near highs, but with some short-term chop, I lean toward continued steepening but temper conviction given the noisy last few prints.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.65pp sustained for multiple readings would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series remains extremely stable and near its 52-week low, closing the latest observation at -0.52, matching the low end of the range. The index has been pinned between -0.50 and -0.52 for the entire 13-week window, with a 4-week change of only -0.02 and a 13-week change of essentially zero. Range position of 0.01 confirms conditions are sitting right at the loosest point of the past year. There is no momentum in either direction — this is a flat, low-volatility regime with financial conditions persistently looser than the historical average (distance from zero of -0.52). My prior flat call was correct: the small wiggle to -0.52 at the end is within the noise band that has characterized the entire period, not a genuine break to renewed easing. Absent any new catalyst (and the latest policy statement is silent on this driver), the most likely outcome for month-end is a continuation of this same tight band around -0.51 to -0.52. I don't see evidence of either a decisive move back above -0.45 (which would signal tightening) or a sustained break meaningfully below -0.52 (which would confirm fresh easing) — the series is essentially oscillating within a 0.02-wide band at its floor.
Would change its mind if: A move back above -0.45 with flat-to-positive 4-week change confirms tightening; a sustained break below -0.52 with negative momentum signals fresh easing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation printed 2.25, the high of the 10-point window, after a stretch of stability around 2.23-2.24. My prior "down" call (conviction 0.40) anticipated continued softening, but the latest reading actually moved up to the top of the recent range, effectively triggering my own stated falsifier (a move back to/above 2.25). That said, it's only one observation, not yet "two consecutive readings" sustained above 2.25, so I should not over-read it. Looking at the broader picture: be_change_21d is now slightly positive (+0.01pp), while be_change_63d and be_change_252d are both -0.10pp, showing the medium/longer-term drift has been down even as the very short-term has ticked up. Breakeven sits at 2.25%, only 0.25pp above the 2% target, well within its 12-month range (range_position 0.28, closer to the low than the high), suggesting no runaway expectations. Given the tension between the longer-term downward drift and the recent uptick back to range highs, I read this as a stabilization rather than a resumption of decline, but not yet strong enough evidence to call a clear reversal upward. I'll shift toward flat, with modest conviction, acknowledging my prior down call was wrong-footed by the latest print.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.22-2.23 sustained over two readings would confirm renewed decline; a sustained push above 2.26 would confirm a genuine upward turn.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "flat" call largely held: headline y/y printed 3.00, essentially unchanged from 2.99, and the m/m gap vs. outgoing (-0.06pp) confirms no acceleration surprise. Headline sits at its 12-month high (yoy_range_position 0.97), having climbed steadily from the 2.33 trough, but the pace of increase has clearly plateaued over the last three prints (2.94, 2.99, 3.00) — a flattening curve rather than a new leg up. Momentum measures are mixed: headline_3m_annualized (3.43%) still runs hotter than the y/y print, suggesting some upward drift risk, but core_3m_annualized (2.40%) is decelerating and core y/y has actually ticked down 0.08pp over three months, with the latest print (2.83) below the recent local peak (2.91). Core running below headline (core_minus_headline -0.17pp) and decelerating is the more important signal for persistence, and it argues against imminent reacceleration in headline. Net: headline is near a local plateau at an elevated level (0.8-1.0pp above target on core), with recent momentum roughly flat-to-slightly-up on headline but cooling on core. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional break either way — most likely essentially unchanged next print, perhaps a marginal uptick given the 3m annualized skew.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or headline y/y above 3.15% would signal renewed acceleration; a drop below 2.85% y/y would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.40% from 4.50%, a decline of 0.10pp — this is the "below 4.60% acceleration threshold" and close to my stated stabilization marker (4.30% or below), though it didn't quite reach that level. My prior "up" call was directionally too aggressive for this print; the rate actually ticked down last month rather than continuing to rise. That said, the broader trend over 12 months is still up (+0.20pp), the 3-month change is still positive (+0.10pp), and the level remains 0.40pp above the 12-month low and 0.40pp above the natural rate estimate, with the Sahm gap holding at 0.43pp — closer to conventional recession-signal territory than to full stabilization. Policy language changes are notable: officials now describe downside employment risks as having "risen in recent months" (upgraded from a static risk assessment), and note "more recent indicators are consistent with" the slowing/upward-edging narrative — suggesting they see continued softening beneath the single-month dip. Given the single-month decline could be noise around a still-elevated and drifting-up base, and the policy tone leans toward further loosening, I lean toward another modest uptick or at least sideways-to-up next report, but with reduced conviction since the immediate print surprised to the downside.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 4.30% would confirm stabilization/reversal; a print above 4.60% would confirm renewed sharp acceleration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_12m, unrate_change_3m, sahm_gap, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted sideways-to-slightly-up over the last month, closing at 4.18%, matching its recent high in this window. My prior "down" call was wrong in direction (though small in magnitude): the 21d change is now +0.09pp and the yield sits at the top of its recent range, not breaking lower toward 3.95 as I'd flagged. The 12m range position (0.26) shows yield still in the lower third of its 12-month band, but the short-term momentum is modestly up. The 30s10s slope at 0.66pp has not cleared my 0.70 steepening threshold, so the strongest upside trigger isn't confirmed, but it's closer than before. Policy language has shifted meaningfully: the Fed ending balance-sheet runoff and now signaling it will conduct purchases of shorter-term Treasuries to maintain reserve levels is a mild net negative for long-end term premium pressure (removes some supply overhang from runoff, and purchases are concentrated short-end, so less direct long-end support). This is a mixed signal — modest upward yield drift on measurements, offset by ballast from the end of QT/runoff. Net: modest, low-conviction lean toward continued slight upward drift or flat, not the down call I made last time.
Would change its mind if: A break below 4.05 with 30s10s flattening toward 0.55 would confirm renewed downward term-premium pressure; a clean push above 4.25 with slope above 0.70 confirms upside.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on pace/size of new short-term Treasury purchases would clarify net duration supply impact on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if flight-to-quality is suppressing or not suppressing long yields.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel has moved against my prior dovish-tilt call in several places, and I need to update accordingly. Labor tightness — my strongest lean last time (+0.55) — actually printed a decline (4.40% from 4.50%), directly hitting my own stated falsifier for the dovish-lower-yields view. The analyst still calls "up" but with reduced conviction (0.30) and admits the single print surprised to the downside; the trend components are mixed at best. This is a real, live repricing signal and it argues for less downward pressure on yields than I had, not more. Inflation stayed flat/plateaued near cycle highs on headline, with core decelerating — a wash, not a driver of direction. Term premium flipped from my prior "down" call to a modest "up" lean (yield drifted to top of its range), though conviction is low and it's partially offset by the end of QT. Balance sheet flipped hard from down to up (QT ending, reserve-management purchases beginning) — a policy-confirmed, fairly clear turn, though it's a slow-moving, largely-anticipated driver whose yield effect (balance sheet expansion is yield-suppressive) is a genuine but modest headwind to yields. Financial conditions remain pinned at multi-week lows, flat, no new information — stale/no-signal, appropriately discounted.

Netting this: the two most trade-relevant, fastest-reacting drivers (labor and inflation) no longer support a confident dovish/lower-yields view — labor's downside surprise removes the main pillar of my prior short-duration position, while inflation is neutral. Term premium's own drift is now mildly upward. Balance-sheet expansion is a genuine but slow offsetting force. On net, the case for staying short duration has weakened materially since last meeting; I should size down toward flat rather than double down or reverse to long.

discounted.why: Flat, low-volatility regime at floor for 13 weeks; no new catalyst, contributes no directional signal.
Plateau near highs but no acceleration surprise; near-neutral, doesn't move the level view materially.

arbitrated.falsifier: A jobs report showing unemployment back above 4.60% or a CPI print above 0.35% m/m would restore the dovish case and justify re-shorting duration; conversely a clean break below 4.05 on the 10y with slope flattening would argue for going long duration.

risks.text: Fed's short-term Treasury purchase program details unknown; could be larger/smaller than priced, swinging long-end supply dynamics.
Labor print could be one-month noise; a reversal back toward 4.50%+ would restore the dovish thesis quickly.
Panel disagreement across drivers (up/flat/up/mixed) means this is a weak level signal; sizing down is appropriate but could still be wrong-footed.
NFCI flatness could mask building stress that resolves suddenly, repricing yields via risk-off flows.

trade.rationale: Labor's downside surprise and term premium's upward drift undercut the prior dovish thesis; balance-sheet expansion offers a modest offsetting pull lower. Cut the short-duration position roughly in half rather than reverse or hold full size.

why.balance_sheet: Multi-week rising streak in assets plus explicit policy language confirms QT-end/reserve purchases; slow driver, largely anticipated.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI pinned in tight band at 52w lows, no momentum either way; stale/no-signal, set to no view.
why.inflation: Headline plateaued near highs, core decelerating; roughly flat/no clear directional push to own measurement.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment ticked DOWN to 4.40% (tighter labor), hitting my prior falsifier; trend still up but conviction low and surprised dovish-negating.
why.term_premium: 10y drifted to top of recent range, 21d change +0.09pp; reverses prior down call but low conviction, offset by QT-end.
